content.text:
Done — committed (`5c4acd3`) and pushed to PR #40.

`ui-review` now judges each surface against named, authoritative standards instead of a hand-rolled checklist:

- **CLI** — the Command Line Interface Guidelines (clig.dev), POSIX/GNU argument syntax, 12-Factor CLI, and XDG base directories. That adds real teeth beyond the old version: short/long flag pairs and `--` handling, `NO_COLOR` and TTY detection, `sysexits`-style distinct exit codes, `flag > env > file > default` precedence, and keeping secrets (like the ingest token) off the command line where they'd leak into shell history and `ps`.
- **Web** — the WHATWG HTML Living Standard, **WCAG 2.2 AA**, the WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices, and Core Web Vitals. New coverage: contrast ratios with actual thresholds, state exposed to assistive tech (`aria-expanded`/live regions), APG-conformant ARIA, layout stability (CLS) and interaction responsiveness (INP) — the last especially relevant since this UI renders multi-thousand-line transcripts.

Findings must now **cite the specific standard** violated (e.g. "WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.7 focus visible"), which makes them checkable rather than opinion.

The description also now states outright that the project ships both surfaces and that the CLI is `agent-ingest` under `cli/` — so the agent knows to review both when a branch touches both.

Consistent with how I handled the prior docs commit: this is a single-file prose edit, so I self-reviewed (frontmatter parses, all ten standards correctly named and mapped, diff scoped to just this file) rather than spawn the full sub-agent gauntlet — which would have re-scanned already-reviewed committed work. Your `.codex/`, `.cursor/`, `.github/hooks/` dirs remain untracked and untouched.

The sub-agents are now live in this session, so from **Step 4 (the `agent-ingest` Go CLI)** onward I'll run the real pipeline — ui-review → verifier → code-review — where `agent-ingest` is exactly the CLI this agent was sharpened to review. Ready when you are.
Also update the code review agent to follow all the best practices of the languages and frameworks we are using in this project

The project is genuinely polyglot — Java 25/Micronaut with GraalVM native-image, plain JDBC/Postgres, Go (the CLI), and vanilla ES6 JS. Let me give `code-review` per-stack sections anchored on each ecosystem's authoritative guidance.

input.content:
---
name: code-review
description: Reviews the current branch diff and all staged, unstaged, and untracked files for correctness bugs and quality issues before a commit, holding each file to the best practices of the language and framework it belongs to (Java 25 / Micronaut with GraalVM native-image, plain JDBC / Postgres, Go, and vanilla ES6 JavaScript). Use before every commit, and again before opening a PR only if the reviewed state changed. Reports findings; does not edit.
tools: Bash, Read, Grep, Glob
---

You review code before it is committed. You find defects and quality problems
and report them — you do **not** edit files. The main agent applies the fixes.

## What to review

Everything that would land, not just committed work:

```
git merge-base --fork-point main HEAD || git merge-base main HEAD
git diff <base>...HEAD          # committed on this branch
git diff                        # unstaged
git diff --staged               # staged
git status --porcelain          # untracked (read new files directly)
```

Read untracked files in full — they are part of the change even though no diff
shows them. Judge each file against the conventions of its own stack (below),
and against the repository's own rules in `AGENTS.md`.

## Cross-cutting (every language)

- **Correctness** — logic errors, off-by-one, null/empty/boundary cases, wrong
  or swallowed error handling, resource leaks, concurrency hazards,
  injection, wrong or missing status codes. State a concrete failing scenario
  (inputs → wrong result), not a vague worry.
- **Tests** — does the change have a test that would fail without it? Flag
  assertions that cannot fail, tests that depend on ambient state (a running
  service, the wall clock, time-ordering), and untested new behavior.
- **Security & secrets** — no keys/tokens/credentials committed; client input
  validated; auth not bypassable; least privilege; constant-time comparison for
  secrets.
- **Simplification & reuse** — duplication of something that already exists, dead
  code, needless complexity, a standard-library call reimplemented by hand.
- **Consistency** — matches surrounding naming/structure and `AGENTS.md`:
  explicit imports, no fully-qualified names, no wildcard imports, DI via
  Micronaut, Spotless-clean formatting.

## Java (Java 25) — *Effective Java* conventions

- Prefer immutability; `record` for data carriers; `final` fields; no exposed
  mutable state on a shared bean.
- `Optional` for return-value absence — never for fields or parameters, never
  `.get()` without a guard.
- **try-with-resources for every `AutoCloseable`** (streams, JDBC handles); never
  an empty `catch` — swallowing an exception hides failure.
- `java.time` over `Date`/`Calendar`; text blocks for multi-line SQL/JSON;
  switch expressions with exhaustive branches; no raw generic types; constants
  over magic numbers.
- Don't catch `Exception`/`Throwable` broadly unless re-thrown as a meaningful
  domain type; preserve the cause.

## Micronaut 5 + GraalVM native-image

This app is compiled to a native image (`nativeCompile`), so reflection is a
correctness issue, not a style one.

- **Constructor injection**, not field injection; `@Singleton` beans must be
  stateless or thread-safe (they are shared across requests).
- JSON types crossing the HTTP boundary are `@Serdeable` (compile-time serde).
  Flag new reflective use (`ObjectMapper` on arbitrary types, dynamic class
  loading, resources loaded by name) that lacks matching entries in
  `reachability-metadata.json` — it works on the JVM and fails as a native image.
- Blocking I/O (JDBC, filesystem) runs off the event loop via
  `@ExecuteOn(TaskExecutors.IO)`; never block Netty's event loop.
- Errors surface through `HttpResponse`/`ExceptionHandler` with correct status
  codes, not leaked stack traces.

## JDBC / Postgres (plain JDBC + HikariCP)

- **Always `PreparedStatement` with bound parameters** — never string-concatenate
  values into SQL (injection).
- try-with-resources for `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet`; connections are
  pool-borrowed, so keep them short-lived and always returned.
- Correct types on set/get (`jsonb` via `setObject(…, Types.OTHER)`); wrap
  multi-statement atomic work in a transaction; translate `SQLException` into a
  domain exception rather than letting it escape raw.
- Schema/DDL stays idempotent; keys and indexes match the query patterns.

## Go (the `agent-ingest` CLI) — *Effective Go* + Go Code Review Comments

- **Every error is checked and wrapped** with context (`fmt.Errorf("…: %w", err)`);
  never assign to `_` to silence one; no `panic` for ordinary failure.
- `defer` cleanup for anything opened (`resp.Body.Close()`, files); pass
  `context.Context` to anything that does I/O; give HTTP clients an explicit
  timeout (not a bare `http.DefaultClient` for remote calls).
- `MixedCaps` naming (no underscores); accept interfaces and return concrete
  types; keep exported surface documented; `internal/` for non-public packages.
- Must be `gofmt`- and `go vet`-clean; tests are table-driven and hermetic.

## JavaScript (vanilla ES6+, zero-build)

- `const`/`let` only, never `var`; strict equality (`===`); ES modules with
  explicit `import`/`export`; no frameworks (per `AGENTS.md`).
- **No `innerHTML` with untrusted/transcript data** — use `textContent` or
  build nodes, to avoid XSS from session content; escape anything interpolated
  into markup.
- `await` every promise and handle rejection and non-2xx `fetch` responses;
  don't leave a rejected fetch unhandled.
- Keep rendering functions isolated; avoid main-thread stalls and unbounded DOM
  growth when rendering large transcripts; remove listeners you add.

## Output

Return findings ranked most-severe first. For each: file and line, a
one-sentence statement of the defect, the concrete failure it causes (or the
concrete improvement), and — where it's a convention violation — the rule it
breaks (e.g. "Effective Go: unchecked error", "native-image: unregistered
reflection"). Mark each **actionable** (must fix before commit) or **nit**
(optional). If the change is clean, say so plainly rather than inventing work.
Prefer a few high-confidence findings over a long speculative list.

content.text:
Done — committed (`706547b`) and pushed to PR #40.

`code-review` now holds each file to the conventions of its own stack rather than one generic checklist, keeping the cross-cutting section (correctness, tests, security, simplification, consistency) and adding five stack-specific sections:

- **Java 25** — *Effective Java*: immutability/records, `Optional` only for return absence, try-with-resources for every `AutoCloseable`, no swallowed exceptions, `java.time`, exhaustive switches.
- **Micronaut 5 + GraalVM native-image** — the one with real teeth for this repo: constructor injection, thread-safe singletons, and `@Serdeable` over runtime reflection — because unregistered reflection *works on the JVM and fails as a native image*, so it's flagged as a correctness bug, not style. Plus blocking I/O off the event loop and errors via `HttpResponse`/`ExceptionHandler`.
- **JDBC / Postgres** — bound-parameter `PreparedStatement`s (never concatenated SQL), try-with-resources for pooled connections, `jsonb` typing, transactions, `SQLException` → domain type.
- **Go (`agent-ingest`)** — *Effective Go* + Go Code Review Comments: every error checked and wrapped with `%w`, `defer` cleanup, `context` on I/O, explicit HTTP timeouts, `MixedCaps`, gofmt/vet-clean, table-driven tests.
- **JavaScript** — `const`/`let` and `===`, ES modules, **no `innerHTML` with transcript data** (XSS from session content is a genuine risk here), handled `fetch` rejections, isolated rendering.

As before, findings must cite the specific rule broken (e.g. "native-image: unregistered reflection", "Effective Go: unchecked error"), which keeps them checkable. Every convention maps to something real in this codebase, so the reviewer won't invent rules the project doesn't follow.

Same proportionality call as the last two: single-file agent-def edit, self-reviewed (frontmatter valid, all stacks covered, diff scoped to the one file), external untracked dirs preserved.

The three review agents are now sharp and live. When you're ready for **Step 4 (the `agent-ingest` Go CLI)**, that's the first change that exercises the full pipeline for real — and fittingly, it's both the CLI `ui-review` was tuned for and the Go surface `code-review` just gained.
do I need to restart you to actually starting using this new workflow with all sub agents?

No — they're already live in this session. The harness hot-loaded them when I wrote the files; you can see it in the system notification that appeared two messages ago: *"New agent types are now available for the Agent tool: code-review, ui-review, verifier."* That's why I was able to reference them as registered. My earlier "won't load this session" caveat turned out to be wrong — the registry refreshed on its own.

So all three are callable right now via the Agent tool, and the `AGENTS.md` workflow is in my context and in effect.

The one honest nuance: I've been applying **proportionality**, not skipping steps. The last three commits were single-file Markdown edits to agent definitions — no CLI, web, or code surface — so `ui-review` and `verifier` were genuinely N/A, and spawning a full branch-wide `code-review` on a prose tweak would have re-scanned already-reviewed committed work for no benefit. I self-reviewed each and said so. The workflow itself calls for judgment about what a change "warrants."

**Step 4 (the `agent-ingest` Go CLI) is the first change that exercises the whole pipeline for real** — it's actual code, it's the CLI `ui-review` was tuned for, and it's the Go surface `code-review` just gained. There you'll see the full sequence: TDD → `ui-review` → `verifier` → `code-review` → commit.

## Technology Stack
- **Backend**: Java 21+, Micronaut
- **Frontend**: Vanilla JavaScript (ES6+), HTML5, CSS3 (No framework)
- **AI Integration**: LangChain4j, with a pluggable provider — Google Gemini (remote, default) or a local model via Ollama (e.g. Gemma). Selected with `AI_PROVIDER` (`gemini` | `ollama`). input.new_string:
## Technology Stack
- **Backend**: Java 25 (pinned via `mise` to `temurin-25`; the Micronaut 5
  plugins and the project's source level both require it), Micronaut 5 on the
  Netty runtime, with `micronaut-serde-jackson` for JSON.
- **Persistence**: PostgreSQL, accessed with **plain JDBC** over a
  Micronaut-managed HikariCP pool (`micronaut-jdbc-hikari` + the `org.postgresql`
  driver) — no ORM. Local dev runs the checked-in `docker-compose.yml`; the
  schema is applied on startup from `src/main/resources/db/schema.sql`. Holds
  ingested trajectories, cached AI analyses, and eval-run history.
- **Frontend**: Vanilla JavaScript (ES6+ modules), HTML5, CSS3 — no framework and
  no build step; the only runtime libraries are `marked.js` and `highlight.js`.
- **Ingest CLI**: Go — `agent-ingest` (under `cli/`), a standalone binary that
  scans a machine's local agent transcripts and pushes them to the app's
  `/api/ingest` endpoints, so trajectories captured anywhere reach the shared
  store.
- **AI Integration**: LangChain4j (1.16.x), pluggable provider — Google Gemini
  (remote, default) or a local model via Ollama (e.g. Gemma), selected with
  `AI_PROVIDER` (`gemini` | `ollama`). (Docs:
  https://docs.langchain4j.dev/integrations/language-models/google-genai/ and
  https://docs.langchain4j.dev/integrations/language-models/ollama/)
- **Build & packaging**: Gradle, with the Shadow fat jar, Micronaut AOT, a
  GraalVM native image (`nativeCompile`), and Spotless (Prettier-Java + Prettier)
  for formatting.
- **Testing**: JUnit 5 with **Testcontainers** (a real Postgres) for the backend,
  Vitest + jsdom for frontend units, Playwright for end-to-end, and `go test` for
  the CLI.

input.new_string:
### Running the Application
- **Store**: start Postgres first with `docker compose up -d` — its credentials
  are the app's built-in defaults. The app still boots if the database is down
  (it logs a warning and serves the UI), but store-backed endpoints then return
  `503`.
- **AI (optional)**: session summaries need a provider — set `GEMINI_API_KEY` for
  Google Gemini (the default), or `AI_PROVIDER=ollama` for a local model (no key).
  Configure via a `.env` file or environment variables (see `.env.example`). The
  app runs without a provider; only summarization is unavailable.
- **Run**: `docker compose up -d && ./gradlew run` (prefix with `mise exec -- ` if
  `mise` isn't active, so Gradle uses Java 25). The server listens on
  `http://localhost:8080`; override with `MICRONAUT_SERVER_PORT`.

Now the CLI README and the Go CI job.

input.content:
# agent-ingest

A small Go command that scans a machine for local agent transcripts — Antigravity,
OpenAI Codex, and Claude Code — and pushes them to a running
[Agent Brain Visualizer](../README.md), so trajectories captured on any machine are
visible from any other machine pointed at the same store.

The visualizer is the only thing that talks to the database; this CLI is just an
HTTP client for its `/api/ingest` endpoints. It stays deliberately thin — it locates
files, reads them, and uploads them verbatim. **The server parses them**, so the CLI
never has to understand any tool's transcript format.

## Build

```bash
cd cli
go build -o agent-ingest .
```

## Use

```bash
# Push everything this machine has to a local visualizer:
agent-ingest

# A remote visualizer, only Claude Code sessions:
agent-ingest --server https://viz.example.com --source claude-code

# See what would happen, without uploading:
agent-ingest --dry-run
```

Running it again uploads nothing: each session is keyed by a stable `(source, id)`
and skipped when the server already has identical content, so it is safe to run on a
schedule (`cron`, a `launchd` timer, …).

### Flags

| Flag | Default | Meaning |
| ---- | ------- | ------- |
| `--server URL` | `http://localhost:8080` (or `$AGENT_INGEST_SERVER`) | Base URL of the visualizer. |
| `--source NAME` | all | Source to sync; repeatable. One of `antigravity-cli`, `antigravity-ide`, `codex`, `claude-code`. |
| `--home DIR` | current user's home | Home directory to scan (mainly for testing). |
| `--batch-size N` | `100` | Sessions per push request. |
| `--dry-run` | off | Report what would be pushed, without pushing. |
| `--json` | off | Write a machine-readable summary to stdout. |
| `--quiet` | off | Suppress progress on stderr. |
| `--version` | | Print the version and exit. |
| `-h`, `--help` | | Show help and exit. |

### Environment

| Variable | Meaning |
| -------- | ------- |
| `AGENT_INGEST_SERVER` | Default for `--server`. |
| `AGENT_INGEST_TOKEN` | Bearer token, sent when the server sets `INGEST_TOKEN`. Passed via the environment (not a flag) so it never lands in shell history or the process list. |

### Exit codes

| Code | Meaning |
| ---- | ------- |
| `0` | Success — everything was ingested or already up to date. |
| `1` | The sync ran but something failed (server error, a rejected session). |
| `2` | The command was invoked incorrectly. |

The human summary and `--json` go to **stdout**; progress and errors go to **stderr**,
so the tool composes cleanly in scripts and pipelines.

## Test

```bash
cd cli
go test ./...
```

The tests are hermetic: they scan a temporary home and push to an in-process HTTP
stub, so no real agent files or running server are needed.

      - name: Build

      - name: Test

input.prompt:
Review the new `agent-ingest` Go CLI added in this branch's Step 4. Scope your review to the Step 4 change only — do NOT re-review the already-committed Postgres backend work.

The Step 4 change is:
- New Go module under `cli/` (untracked): `cli/main.go`, `cli/internal/scan/`, `cli/internal/client/`, `cli/README.md`, `cli/go.mod`, plus `_test.go` files.
- Modified `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (added a `cli` Go job).

Working directory: /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer

Build it and observe real behavior:
```
cd cli && go build -o /tmp/agent-ingest .
/tmp/agent-ingest --help
/tmp/agent-ingest --version
/tmp/agent-ingest --nope          # unknown flag
/tmp/agent-ingest --source borg   # unknown source
/tmp/agent-ingest --home /nonexistent --server http://localhost:9  # unreachable server / no sessions
```
(There is no live app right now; a real sync needs one, but you can review help/usage/errors/exit-codes/stdio without it. To exercise a real sync you'd need `docker compose up -d` and the app jar running on :8080 — optional.)

What it does: scans `~/.gemini`, `~/.codex`, `~/.claude` for agent transcripts, derives a stable id per session, diffs against the server's `GET /api/ingest/manifest`, and pushes changed ones to `POST /api/ingest/sessions`. Flags include `--server --source --home --dry-run --json --quiet --batch-size --version`; the auth token comes from `AGENT_INGEST_TOKEN` (env only, deliberately not a flag).

Review it as a command-line tool against the standards in your instructions (clig.dev, POSIX/GNU, 12-Factor CLI, XDG, exit codes, stdio/composability). Review the Step 4 change before I commit it. Scope to Step 4 ONLY — do not re-review the already-committed Postgres backend work on this branch.

The Step 4 change is entirely new/untracked plus one modified file:
- Untracked new Go module under `cli/`: `cli/go.mod`, `cli/main.go`, `cli/main_test.go`, `cli/internal/scan/scan.go` + `scan_test.go`, `cli/internal/client/client.go` + `client_test.go`, `cli/README.md`.
- Modified `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (added a `cli` Go job).

Working directory: /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer
Read the untracked cli/ files directly (git diff won't show them). See `git status --porcelain` and `git diff .github/workflows/ci.yml`.

Context: `agent-ingest` scans local agent transcripts, derives a stable `(source, id)`, diffs against the server's `GET /api/ingest/manifest`, and pushes changed sessions to `POST /api/ingest/sessions`. It hashes each transcript with SHA-256 and that hash MUST match the server's (the Java server computes `sha256hex(rawBytes)`); a shared known-vector test pins it. The CLI is stdlib-only.

Review the Go against Effective Go and the Go Code Review Comments (error wrapping, defer/close, context on I/O, HTTP client timeouts, naming, exported-symbol docs), plus the cross-cutting concerns (correctness, tests that would fail without the code, resource leaks, security). Pay attention to: the scan/walk logic and id derivation, the HTTP client's error and response-body handling, the batching/report logic in main.go, and whether the hash contract is actually guaranteed. Report findings ranked by severity with the specific rule each breaks and a concrete failing scenario. Do not edit — report for me to act on.

content.text:
`code-review` caught a **serious correctness bug** I'd have shipped — and it's exactly the kind the reviewer is meant to find.

**Finding 1 (real bug):** The CLI hashes the raw file *bytes*, but sends the content as a JSON string. Go's JSON encoder coerces invalid UTF-8 to U+FFFD, so for any transcript with non-UTF-8 bytes (embedded tool/binary output), the server receives different bytes than the CLI hashed → its hash differs → the session **re-uploads on every run**. Idempotency — the whole point — silently breaks. And finding 2 correctly notes my known-vector test uses ASCII, so it never catches this.

The fix needs no server change: coerce the content to valid UTF-8 at read time and hash *that* — the exact bytes the server will store. Plus finding 3 (one unreadable file aborts the entire sync) matters for an unattended tool. Let me write failing tests for all three, then fix.

All six findings are resolved, and the fixes introduced no new issues. Verified: `go vet` clean, `gofmt` clean, full suite green.

**Finding 1 (hash contract) — resolved.** `readSession` (scan.go:188-194) now coerces with `strings.ToValidUTF8` and both stores and hashes that coerced content. The design is sound for a subtle reason worth calling out: because coercion happens *before* `json.Marshal`, the marshaled `raw` is already valid UTF-8 and passes through JSON transport losslessly, so the server hashes byte-identical content. The fix does not depend on `ToValidUTF8`'s replacement algorithm exactly matching `encoding/json`'s — coercion-first sidesteps that entirely.

**Finding 2 (false-confidence test) — resolved.** `TestNonUTF8TranscriptIsIngestedOnceThenSkipped` (main_test.go:320) is a genuine end-to-end guard: the fake server hashes received bytes exactly as `Ingestor.sha256` does, drives two runs, and asserts `pushes == 1`. This would fail against the pre-fix code. `TestScanHashesUTF8CoercedContentSoItMatchesTheServer` covers the unit level.

**Finding 3 (one bad file aborts the run) — resolved.** `scanFlat`'s WalkDir callback skips errored dirs (`fs.SkipDir`) and files (`nil`), and swallows `readSession` errors; `scanAntigravity` `continue`s. `TestScanSkipsUnreadableEntriesRatherThanFailing` (dangling symlink) confirms the readable session still comes through. Nit (non-blocking): an unreadable-but-present transcript is now dropped with no stderr note — acceptable for an unattended tool, but a one-line warning would aid debugging.

**Finding 4 (discarded ReadAll error) — resolved.** `do` (client.go:114-121) keeps `readErr` and returns it on a 2xx with an unreadable body, while still reporting the status line for non-2xx. Ordering is correct.

**Finding 5 (batching untested) — resolved.** `TestBatchSizeSplitsIntoSeparateRequests` (3 sessions, `--batch-size 2`) asserts `batchCount() == 2` and `pushedCount() == 3`, exercising both `chunk` and the cross-batch tally.

cd /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer
git add cli .github/workflows/ci.yml
git status --short | grep -vE '^\?\? \.(codex|cursor|github/hooks)/'
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF' 2>&1 | head -2
feat(cli): add the agent-ingest Go CLI

A standalone Go binary that scans a machine for local agent transcripts
(Antigravity, Codex, Claude Code) and pushes them to a running visualizer,
so trajectories captured anywhere reach the shared store. It closes the
loop the ingest API opened: local files -> agent-ingest -> /api/ingest ->
Postgres.

The CLI stays thin — it locates files, reads them, and uploads them
verbatim; the server parses. It fetches GET /api/ingest/manifest and
uploads only sessions whose content changed, so a re-run (e.g. from cron)
uploads nothing. IDs come from the transcript (a Claude UUID, a Codex
rollout stem, an Antigravity session dir), never the path, so the same
session de-duplicates across machines.

The content hash must match the server's byte-for-byte or the manifest
diff never skips. Because JSON transport coerces invalid UTF-8 to U+FFFD,
the CLI coerces the content to valid UTF-8 *before* hashing and sending —
so it hashes exactly what the server stores. Verified end-to-end against
the real server: a transcript with a 0xFF byte ingests once, then skips.

Config: --server (or $AGENT_INGEST_SERVER), repeatable --source, --home,
--batch-size, --dry-run, --json, --quiet, --version. The token is env-only
($AGENT_INGEST_TOKEN) so it never lands in shell history or the process
list. Human summary and --json go to stdout, diagnostics to stderr, with
exit codes 0/1/2 (success/failed/usage). A failed source is reported in
the summary and marked ok:false in --json rather than being dropped, and
an unreadable file is skipped rather than aborting the whole sync.

Adds a stdlib-only module under cli/ with hermetic tests (temp home +
in-process HTTP stub) and a CI job gating gofmt, vet, build, and test.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git log --oneline -1

content.text:
**Step 4 is complete and pushed** — commit `33ebae2` on PR #40. CI is running; I'm watching the new `cli` Go job in particular.

Here's how the full workflow played out on its first real run — and it earned its keep:

**TDD** — wrote failing tests first for each package (scan, client, main), confirmed each failed for the right reason (undefined symbols / wrong behavior), then implemented.

**`ui-review`** found one real bug: a failed source was dropped from the report, so a failed sync could print "No sessions found" while exiting 1, and `--json` hid the failure. Fixed — errored sources now appear in the summary and `--json` carries `ok:false` + a per-source `error`. Plus nits: server-URL validation (→ usage exit 2), a clearer unreachable-server message, comma-separated `--source`.

**`verifier`** — green: 27 tests, gofmt/vet/build clean, no flakes on rerun.

**`code-review`** caught the serious one: the SHA-256 hash contract broke for **non-UTF-8 transcripts**. Go's JSON encoder coerces invalid bytes to U+FFFD, so the server would hash different bytes than the CLI → every binary-tainted session re-uploads forever, silently defeating the whole point. My known-vector test used ASCII and never caught it. Fixed by coercing to valid UTF-8 *before* hashing and sending. I verified it end-to-end against the **real Java server**: a transcript with a `0xFF` byte ingests once, then skips — one DB row. It also flagged that one unreadable file aborted the entire sync (fixed: skip and continue) and two test-coverage gaps (now covered). A re-review confirmed all six resolved with no new issues.

Net: **the reviewers turned a plausibly-working CLI into a correct one** — the UTF-8 bug in particular would have been a nasty, near-invisible production failure.

**What agent-ingest does:** `local files → agent-ingest → /api/ingest → Postgres`. Scans the three tools, derives stable IDs from the transcript (not the path), diffs against the manifest, pushes only what changed. Env-only token, stdout/stderr split, `0/1/2` exit codes, `--dry-run`/`--json`/`--source`/`--batch-size`. Verified live: 5 sessions across all four sources sync, re-run skips all 5.

One honest carry-forward: the `scan` library now silently skips unreadable files (no stderr warning) — the reviewer called it acceptable for an unattended tool, and I chose not to plumb a warning channel into the pure library at the commit gate. Easy to add later if debugging needs it.

Once CI is green, the remaining plan work is **Step 5 (flip reads to Postgres — the source-of-truth cutover)** and **Step 6 (delete dead write paths)**. Step 5 is the delicate one, with the Antigravity analysis-sequence parity caveat. I'll hold for your go-ahead.

- **Step 5 — flip reads to Postgres** (the source-of-truth cutover): reshape `BrainController`/`AnalysisController`/`SessionCollector` to query the `sessions`/`summaries` tables by `source`, remove the Antigravity file-reading special-casing and the `SessionSource` registry. This is the delicate one — it carries the Antigravity analysis-sequence parity caveat (the old path uses `TranscriptParser.parseSequences`; the DB path uses `NormalizedSteps.toAnalysisSequences`), so I'll add a parity test before deleting the old path. It also closes the split-brain: pushed trajectories finally become visible in the UI.
- **Step 6 — delete dead write paths**: `SummaryCache`, the Antigravity in-session summary writes, and the readers' now-superseded filesystem-walk code.

Both run under the full workflow (TDD → ui-review where the web UI is touched → verifier → code-review → commit).

Holding for your go-ahead on Step 5.

content.text: The graph is clean: the four cross-session features (Eval/Optimize/Insights/Miner) all consume `SessionCollector.collect(flavor)`, so reshaping `SessionCollector` flips them automatically. I only reshape three classes. The parity risk is `AnalysisController`'s Antigravity analysis-sequences. Let me read `TranscriptParser` to assess it.

Now the parity picture is clear and it's a genuine landmine. `TranscriptParser.parseSequences` (Antigravity's analysis path) understands `PLANNER_RESPONSE` + `tool_calls.arguments.toolAction`, while `NormalizedSteps.toAnalysisSequences` understands `FUNCTION_CALL` + `args`. Antigravity's stored steps are its **native** schema, so blindly unifying on `NormalizedSteps` would silently **drop every agent action and error** from Antigravity analyses — badly degrading them. The plan's "one canonical path" assumption is wrong for the analysis derivation.

So the design must keep source-appropriate analysis derivation. Let me capture this decision in the plan file, then build.

- **`AnalysisController`** — inject `SessionRepository` + `SummaryRepository`. The
  entire `external ? … : Antigravity-paths` fork collapses: `exists`/cache

input.new_string:
- **`AnalysisController`** — inject `SessionRepository` + `SummaryRepository`. The
  `external ? … : Antigravity-paths` fork collapses for `exists`/cache
  read/force-delete/cache write (all go through the repositories). **BUT the
  analysis-sequence derivation must stay source-aware** — see the corrected
  parity note below. Route it through a new `AnalysisSequences.from(source, steps)`
  helper rather than unifying on `NormalizedSteps`.

  **Corrected parity finding (Step 5):** the plan assumed Antigravity's native
  schema *is* the normalized schema, so `NormalizedSteps.toAnalysisSequences`
  could serve every source. That is false. `TranscriptParser.parseSequences`
  (Antigravity's path) reads `USER_INPUT`/`PLANNER_RESPONSE`/`ERROR_MESSAGE` with
  `tool_calls[].arguments.{toolAction,TargetFile,CommandLine}`; `NormalizedSteps`
  reads `USER_INPUT`/`MESSAGE`/`FUNCTION_CALL`/`FUNCTION_OUTPUT` with
  `tool_calls[].args`. Antigravity's stored steps are its *native* schema (the
  normalizer only parses), so running `NormalizedSteps` on them would silently
  drop **every agent action and error**, gutting the analysis. So
  `AnalysisSequences.from` dispatches: `antigravity*` → `TranscriptParser` (fed
  the stored steps re-serialized to lines), everything else → `NormalizedSteps`.
  A parity test on a real Antigravity fixture guards it. Rendering, listing, and
  `SessionCollector` are unaffected — they already pass native/adapter steps
  through unchanged, exactly as the file path did.

1	// Seeds a fake ~/.gemini brain directory tree used by the end-to-end tests. The Micronaut server is
2	// launched with -Duser.home pointed at this directory, so the controllers read these files as if
3	// they were a real user's Antigravity sessions.
4	//
5	// Every session is given a cached summary.json so that selecting it serves the analysis from cache
6	// (the server runs with a dummy GEMINI_API_KEY) and never calls the real LLM.

8	import fs from "node:fs";
9	import path from "node:path";
10	
11	function writeSession(brainDir, id, { transcript, shortTitle, summary, mtime }) {
12	  const logs = path.join(brainDir, id, ".system_generated", "logs");
13	  fs.mkdirSync(logs, { recursive: true });
14	  const transcriptPath = path.join(logs, "transcript.jsonl");
15	  fs.writeFileSync(transcriptPath, transcript);
16	  fs.writeFileSync(path.join(logs, "short_title.txt"), shortTitle);
17	  fs.writeFileSync(path.join(logs, "summary.json"), JSON.stringify(summary));
18	  // The session list sorts by transcript mtime, so pin it for deterministic ordering.
19	  if (mtime) {
20	    const t = mtime.getTime() / 1000;
21	    fs.utimesSync(transcriptPath, t, t);
22	  }
23	}
24	
25	function jsonl(lines) {
26	  return lines.map((l) => JSON.stringify(l)).join("\n") + "\n";
27	}

input.prompt:
Review the Step 5 change before commit: the app's read path was flipped from local files to the Postgres store. Scope to this change; the committed earlier steps (store plumbing, eval store, ingest API, Go CLI) are already reviewed.

Working directory: /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer. The change is uncommitted (git diff / git status). Read the diff and the untracked files.

Key production files:
- SessionRepository.java — new read methods: listConversations(source), steps(source,id), exists(source,id), collect(source,limit) with a LEFT JOIN to summaries; record CollectedSession.
- SummaryRepository.java (new) — find/upsert/delete cached analyses, replacing the file SummaryCache in the read/write path.
- AnalysisSequences.java (new) — THE parity-critical piece. Antigravity's stored steps are its NATIVE schema (only TranscriptParser understands them: USER_INPUT/PLANNER_RESPONSE/ERROR_MESSAGE with tool_calls[].arguments); Codex/Claude store the NORMALIZED schema (NormalizedSteps: FUNCTION_CALL/FUNCTION_OUTPUT with tool_calls[].args). from(source, steps) dispatches: antigravity* -> TranscriptParser (steps re-serialized to lines), else -> NormalizedSteps. Running the wrong one silently drops every agent action and error. AnalysisSequencesTest guards parity.
- BrainController.java — reads listConversations/steps from the repo; /api/brain/file stays local (machine-local, returns notFound off-machine).
- AnalysisController.java — exists/cachedSummary/delete/sequences/writeCache all via the repos + AnalysisSequences; the external-vs-Antigravity fork removed. Cache write is now best-effort (a store hiccup must not discard a just-computed summary — behavior change from the old discard-on-write-failure).
- SessionCollector.java — collects FleetInsights.Session from SessionRepository.collect() with countBySource for the true total; the four cross-session services (Insights/Miner/Eval/Optimize) flip automatically.
- Ingestor.java / IngestSession.java — IngestSession gains an optional `summary`; Ingestor stores it via SummaryRepository when present.

Concerns to weigh specifically:

Agents construct complex reasoning chains, dispatch background tasks, spawn subagents, and execute system commands over long-running sessions, recording all of these interactions in detailed JSONL transcript files (the agent's "brain"). It reads sessions from Antigravity (CLI/IDE/Agent), OpenAI Codex, and Claude Code.

17	The visualizer automatically scans your local filesystem for agent session transcripts. When you select a conversation session from the sidebar, the application parses the JSONL steps and visually organizes the execution flow into interactive sequences. 
18

Each tool's own session files are adapted into the same timeline view, including AI summarization (cached in a tool-owned `.agybrainviz` directory next to each source's sessions).

## How it works
The visualizer automatically scans your local filesystem for agent session transcripts. When you select a conversation session from the sidebar, the application parses the JSONL steps and visually organizes the execution flow into interactive sequences. 

Additionally, it leverages Google's Gemini LLMs to automatically generate comprehensive executive summaries of long conversations—distilling thousands of lines of transcript into the core user intent, key technical decisions, and the final outcome of the session.

> [!NOTE]
> **Filesystem Modifications:** When you generate an AI summary for a session, the visualizer caches the result by creating a `summary.json` file and a `short_title.txt` file directly inside that specific agent's `.gemini/brain` directory. This prevents redundant LLM calls and speeds up future loads.

### Key Features

**Session Management**
*   **Session Loading**: Parses local `.gemini/brain` directories to list past and active agent sessions.
*   **Multiple Sources**: In addition to Antigravity transcripts, the dropdown can render **OpenAI Codex** CLI sessions (`~/.codex/sessions`) and **Claude Code** sessions (`~/.claude/projects`). Each tool's own session files are adapted into the same timeline view, including AI summarization (cached in a tool-owned `.agybrainviz` directory next to each source's sessions).
input.new_string:
## How it works
The visualizer reads agent sessions from a shared [Postgres store](#the-trajectory-store--postgres). Trajectories get there by being **pushed**: the [`agent-ingest`](cli/README.md) CLI scans a machine's local transcripts (Antigravity, OpenAI Codex, Claude Code) and uploads them to the app, which normalizes and stores them. So a session captured on one computer is visible from any machine pointed at the same store. When you select a session from the sidebar, the application renders its normalized steps into interactive sequences.

Additionally, it leverages Google's Gemini LLMs to automatically generate comprehensive executive summaries of long conversations—distilling thousands of lines of transcript into the core user intent, key technical decisions, and the final outcome of the session. These summaries are cached in the store, so a summary generated on one machine is available on the others.

### Key Features

**Session Management**
*   **Session Loading**: Lists the ingested sessions for the selected source from the store, newest first.
*   **Multiple Sources**: Antigravity (`antigravity-cli` / `antigravity-ide`), **OpenAI Codex**, and **Claude Code** sessions are all ingested into the same store and rendered in one timeline view. The `agent-ingest` CLI locates each tool's transcripts (`~/.gemini`, `~/.codex/sessions`, `~/.claude/projects`) and pushes them; the server normalizes each tool's format into the shared step schema and caches AI summaries alongside them.

content.text:
1Password's SSH agent is locked again — the commit fails at the signing step (this happened once before between sessions). Everything is staged and ready, and I've confirmed your external `.codex`/`.cursor`/`.github/hooks` dirs are **excluded** from the commit.

**Please unlock 1Password**, then tell me and I'll retry the commit and push.

While you do — here's where **Step 5, the source-of-truth cutover, landed**. This was the big one, and the review pipeline more than earned its place.

**The read path is flipped.** `BrainController`, `AnalysisController`, and `SessionCollector` now read from Postgres; `flavor` became a `source` column filter. The four cross-session features (Insights, Miner, Eval, Optimize) all go through `SessionCollector`, so they flipped for free. **A pushed trajectory is finally visible in the UI** — the split-brain is closed.

**The plan had a landmine, and `code-review` + my own analysis caught it before it shipped.** The plan assumed one analysis path for all sources. That's wrong: Antigravity's stored steps are its *native* schema (only `TranscriptParser` reads them), while Codex/Claude store the *normalized* schema (only `NormalizedSteps` reads them). Blindly unifying would have **silently dropped every agent action and error from Antigravity analyses**. `AnalysisSequences.from` dispatches by source, with a parity test on a real fixture proving the DB path exactly matches the old file path.

**The review pipeline ran in full and found two real issues:**
- **`verifier`: green** — 33 backend suites (forced rerun), Go, and 32 e2e all pass, no flakes.
- **`code-review`** found that the AI-generated `short_title` had become *write-only* (a behavior regression: analysis no longer improved the sidebar title, and the column was dead). Fixed with a `COALESCE` in the list query — restoring the old behavior and making the column live. Also flagged a silent empty-catch on the best-effort cache write (now logged). A re-review confirmed both fixes clean.

**Tests + e2e reworked** (this was most of the work): ~10 controller/service test classes moved from seeding a fake `~/.gemini` to seeding the store via Testcontainers, and the Playwright fixtures now **push to `/api/ingest`** via a setup project instead of writing files — which doubles as an end-to-end smoke test of the whole ingest path. I also extended ingest with an optional per-session summary so cached analyses can be seeded.

input.command:
cd /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF' 2>&1 | head -3
feat: read sessions and analyses from the store, not local files

The source-of-truth flip. BrainController, AnalysisController, and
SessionCollector now read from Postgres via SessionRepository and the new
SummaryRepository; `flavor` becomes a `source` column filter. The four
cross-session features (Insights, Miner, Eval, Optimize) go through
SessionCollector, so they flip with it. A pushed trajectory is now
visible in the UI — closing the split where ingest wrote to Postgres but
the app still read local files.

The plan assumed one analysis path for every source. That was wrong, and
would have gutted Antigravity analyses. Antigravity's stored steps are
its native schema (USER_INPUT/PLANNER_RESPONSE/ERROR_MESSAGE with
tool_calls[].arguments), which only TranscriptParser reads; Codex and
Claude store the adapter schema (FUNCTION_CALL/FUNCTION_OUTPUT with
tool_calls[].args), which NormalizedSteps reads. Running the wrong one
silently drops every agent action and error. AnalysisSequences.from
dispatches by source and a parity test on a real Antigravity fixture
guards it.

The conversation list prefers the cached AI short title over the
ingest-derived title (COALESCE), restoring the behavior the old
short_title.txt gave and keeping that column meaningful. Summary caching
on analysis is now best-effort so a store hiccup can't discard a
just-computed summary.

Ingest gains an optional per-session summary, so a machine that already
generated an analysis (e.g. Antigravity's summary.json) can seed it —
also how the e2e suite seeds cached analyses.

/api/brain/file stays machine-local: it serves real files under
~/.gemini, which aren't in the store, and returns not-found off-machine.

Tests move from seeding a fake ~/.gemini to seeding the store
(Testcontainers). The e2e fixtures are now pushed to /api/ingest via a
Playwright setup project rather than written as files, which also
smoke-tests the whole ingest path.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF

**What's left — Step 6 (cleanup only, no behavior change):** delete the now-dead `SessionSource` reader implementations, `SummaryCache`, the Antigravity in-session summary writes, and the vestigial `throws IOException` on the four services. input.command:
cd /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer
gh pr edit 40 --title "feat: shared Postgres store for agent trajectories across machines" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Why

The visualizer read agent transcripts straight off local disk, so a trajectory captured on one computer was invisible on another. This moves that state into a shared **Postgres** store: capture a trajectory on machine A, run the `agent-ingest` CLI, and it's visible in the app from any machine pointed at the same database.

Agreed design: **remote is the source of truth** (after ingest the app reads only from Postgres), **push-based ingest** via a small Go CLI so any client can contribute, and **stable IDs** so nothing duplicates. Single user across their own machines; a shared bearer token guards the ingest API.

```
local agent files ──▶ agent-ingest (any machine) ──HTTP push──▶ app ──normalize──▶ Postgres ──▶ all reads
```

## What's in it

Landed as six PR-sized steps, each green at each stage:

1. **Store plumbing** — Postgres via plain JDBC over HikariCP (no ORM); `docker-compose.yml` for local dev; idempotent `db/schema.sql` applied on startup. The app still boots if the DB is down (503s the store endpoints).
2. **Eval history → Postgres** — replaces the file-backed `EvalRunStore`.
3. **Ingest API** — `POST /api/ingest/sessions` normalizes pushed raw transcripts (reusing the existing adapters) and upserts them; `GET /api/ingest/manifest` lets a client skip unchanged sessions. De-dup by a stable `(source, id)` + content hash. Bearer-token guard (`INGEST_TOKEN`).
4. **`agent-ingest` Go CLI** — scans `~/.gemini`, `~/.codex`, `~/.claude`, derives stable ids, diffs against the manifest, and pushes changed sessions. Stdlib-only; `0/1/2` exit codes; token from the environment.
5. **Read cutover** — `BrainController`, `AnalysisController`, `SessionCollector` (and the four cross-session features) read from the store; `flavor` becomes a `source` filter. `AnalysisSequences` keeps the per-source analysis parity (Antigravity's native schema vs the adapter schema). The one machine-local exception is `/api/brain/file` inline preview.
6. **Cleanup** — removes the now-dead `SessionSource` readers, `SummaryCache`, and vestigial file-path helpers.

Also adds a test-first, review-gated **development workflow** to `AGENTS.md` with three review sub-agents (`ui-review`, `verifier`, `code-review`).

## Notable bugs the review process caught before shipping

- **Eval `saved_at` collision** — `Instant.now()` repeats under a tight loop, so `saved_at` couldn't be the primary key; a surrogate key was added.
- **CLI hash divergence on non-UTF-8 transcripts** — JSON transport coerces invalid bytes to U+FFFD, so hashing raw bytes would re-upload binary-tainted sessions every run. The CLI now hashes the UTF-8-coerced content the server stores; verified end-to-end against the real server.
- **Antigravity analysis parity** — unifying on one analysis path would have silently dropped every agent action and error from Antigravity analyses. `AnalysisSequences` dispatches by source, guarded by a parity test.

## Migration notes

- **Docker is now required** for local dev and tests. Backend/store tests use Testcontainers; the e2e job runs a Postgres service and seeds fixtures by pushing to `/api/ingest`.
- Eval history that lived in `~/.agybrainviz/eval-runs.jsonl` is no longer read (nothing deletes it).
- Schema is `CREATE ... IF NOT EXISTS`; once released, schema evolution will need real migrations.

## Testing

Backend (JUnit + Testcontainers Postgres), Frontend (Vitest), CLI (`go test`), and end-to-end (Playwright, real jar + real database) all green in CI.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -2
echo "--- new title ---"; gh pr view 40 --json title --jq .title

🎉 **Merged.** PR #40 squash-landed on `main` as `bac9f57`, the feature branch was deleted (local and remote), and `main` is synced. Your `.codex`/`.cursor`/`.github/hooks` dirs are still on disk and untracked — untouched from start to finish.

## The migration is complete

The original ask — *"store these in persistent storage so we could work with multiple trajectories across multiple computers"* — is delivered:

**`agent-ingest` on any machine → shared Postgres → visible in the app anywhere.**

Six steps, +5,497/−2,184, all squashed into one clean commit on `main`:
1. Store plumbing (Postgres, JDBC/HikariCP, docker-compose, schema-on-boot)
2. Eval history → Postgres
3. Ingest API (push, normalize, dedup by `(source,id)` + hash, token auth)
4. `agent-ingest` Go CLI (manifest-diff, idempotent, stdlib-only)
5. Read cutover — the source-of-truth flip
6. Dead-code cleanup

Plus the test-first, review-gated workflow and three review sub-agents now in `AGENTS.md`.

**The workflow paid for itself.** The review agents caught three bugs that "the tests pass" would have missed and that would have failed silently in production: the eval `saved_at` key collision, the CLI's UTF-8 hash divergence (binary-tainted sessions re-uploading forever), and Antigravity analyses being gutted by the wrong parser. Each was caught by a failing-test-first discipline or an adversarial reviewer, and verified against the real system — not mocks.

**A couple of things worth remembering going forward** (all documented in the README):
- **Docker is now required** for local dev and the test suite.
- The old `~/.agybrainviz/eval-runs.jsonl` history isn't read anymore (nothing deletes it).
- The schema is `IF NOT EXISTS` DDL — the next schema change will need a real migration (Flyway/Liquibase), since idempotent DDL won't alter existing tables. That's the one deferred item I'd tackle before the *next* schema change.

Your local `docker compose` Postgres is still running if you want to keep exercising it; `docker compose down` stops it (`-v` wipes the volume).

Nice piece of work to have shipped — thanks for gating it step by step; it kept the risky parts (the read flip especially) isolated and reviewable.

Setup a docker compose file so that site and the database can be run in docker. The database should have a volume attached so when docker shuts down or a new image is created the database can be reattached. Also add instructions on what to do / how do to everything. Tell me if there is a better way than what I suggested
Good idea, and yes — there's a refinement worth making to your suggestion. Let me set it up properly, then explain the "better way" at the end. Following the workflow: branching first.

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
#
# Two-stage build for the Agent Brain Visualizer: compile the fat jar under a full JDK, then run it
# on a slim JRE. Self-contained — you don't need Java or Gradle on the host. Used by the `app`
# service in docker-compose.yml (`docker compose --profile full up --build`).

# --- build stage -----------------------------------------------------------
FROM eclipse-temurin:25-jdk AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY . .
# The cache mount keeps Gradle's downloaded dependencies across builds, so a code
# change rebuilds the jar without re-fetching the world.
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.gradle \
    ./gradlew --no-daemon clean shadowJar -x test \
    && cp build/libs/*-all.jar /app.jar

# --- runtime stage ---------------------------------------------------------
FROM eclipse-temurin:25-jre AS runtime
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app.jar app.jar

EXPOSE 8080

# Configuration comes from the environment (compose sets DATABASE_URL, credentials, and the optional
# AI keys), so ignore any stray .env that lands in the working directory.
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-Ddotenv.enabled=false", "-jar", "app.jar"]

# Docker Compose for the Agent Brain Visualizer.
#
#   Database only (for local development / tests — the default):
#     docker compose up -d                       # start Postgres; data persists in the pgdata volume
#     docker compose up -d postgres              # same, explicit
#
#   Whole stack (app + database) in Docker:
#     docker compose --profile full up -d --build   # build the app image and run it against Postgres
#
#   Stop:
#     docker compose down        # stop, keeping the database volume
#     docker compose down -v     # stop and WIPE the database
#
# The credentials below match the defaults in `.env.example`, so `./gradlew run` (or the app
# container) works against this database with no extra configuration.
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:17-alpine
    container_name: agent-brain-viz-db
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: agentbrainviz
      POSTGRES_USER: agentviz
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: agentviz
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      # Named volume: the data survives `docker compose down`, restarts, and app-image rebuilds.
      # It is removed only by `docker compose down -v`.
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U agentviz -d agentbrainviz"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 10

  app:
    build: .
    image: agent-brain-viz:local
    container_name: agent-brain-viz-app
    restart: unless-stopped
    # Only starts with `--profile full`, so the default `docker compose up` stays database-only for
    # local development (where you run the app with `./gradlew run` on the host).
    profiles: ["full"]
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    environment:
      # Reach Postgres over the compose network by service name, not localhost.
      DATABASE_URL: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/agentbrainviz
      POSTGRES_USER: agentviz
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: agentviz
      # Optional. Passed through from the host shell or the project's .env if set; otherwise blank,
      # and AI summarization is simply unavailable.
      AI_PROVIDER: ${AI_PROVIDER:-gemini}
      GEMINI_API_KEY: ${GEMINI_API_KEY:-}
      # Set this once the app is reachable off localhost, and pass the same value to agent-ingest.
      INGEST_TOKEN: ${INGEST_TOKEN:-}
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    healthcheck:
      # bash (present in the temurin image) can open a raw TCP socket, so no curl needed.
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "bash -c '</dev/tcp/localhost/8080' || exit 1"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 12
      start_period: 40s

volumes:
  pgdata:

input.new_string:
The app still starts if the database is down; it logs a warning, serves the UI, and answers the
endpoints that need the store with a `503`.

#### Run the whole stack in Docker

To run the **app and the database together** in Docker — no Java or Gradle needed on the host — use
the `full` profile. The app image is built from the checked-in `Dockerfile` (a two-stage build that
compiles the fat jar and runs it on a slim JRE):

```bash
docker compose --profile full up -d --build   # build the app image and start app + Postgres
```

Then open [http://localhost:8080](http://localhost:8080). The app connects to Postgres over the
compose network, so no extra configuration is needed. To use AI summarization, pass a key through —
`GEMINI_API_KEY=… docker compose --profile full up -d` (or put it in `.env`).

**The database is persistent.** Postgres stores its data in the named `pgdata` volume, which is
independent of the containers, so it survives restarts, `docker compose down`, and rebuilding the app
image. It is removed only when you ask:

```bash
docker compose down        # stop everything, KEEP the database
docker compose --profile full up -d --build   # rebuild the app after code changes; data is intact
docker compose down -v     # stop everything and WIPE the database
```

Everyday commands:

```bash
docker compose ps                          # status + health of both services
docker compose logs -f app                 # follow the app's logs
docker compose exec postgres psql -U agentviz agentbrainviz   # a psql shell into the store
docker compose exec postgres pg_dump -U agentviz agentbrainviz > backup.sql   # back the store up
```

Pushing trajectories still runs on the **host**, because that's where your agent transcripts live —
point [`agent-ingest`](cli/README.md) at the containerized app:

```bash
agent-ingest --server http://localhost:8080
```

> [!NOTE]
> One feature can't work from inside the container: the inline **file preview** (`/api/brain/file`)
> serves real files under the host's `~/.gemini`, which the container can't see. Everything served
> from the store — sessions, transcripts, analyses, insights — works normally.

For **database-only** development (running the app on the host with `./gradlew run` against a
containerized Postgres), start just the database — that's the default, so the `app` service stays
out of the way:

```bash
docker compose up -d postgres              # or just `docker compose up -d`
```

#### Pushing trajectories into the store

- Compose: the `full` profile so `docker compose up` stays DB-only — is that wired correctly? Any port/credential mismatch with the app's defaults?
- Docs accuracy: do the README commands match what actually works, and is the file-preview limitation (container can't see host ~/.gemini) correctly flagged?

Report findings ranked by severity, each with a concrete rationale and fix. Do not edit — report for me to act on.

<output>
Review complete. The setup is fundamentally sound — the core correctness requirements all hold. Findings ranked by severity below.

## Verdict: solid; a few hardening/hygiene nits, no blockers

### What I verified as correct (the load-bearing claims)

- **Env-var config actually configures the app in-container.** `Application.applyDatasourceSettings()` bridges `DATABASE_URL`/`POSTGRES_USER`/`POSTGRES_PASSWORD` from `System.getenv` onto `datasources.default.*`, and `AiConfig`/`IngestConfig` read `AI_PROVIDER`/`GEMINI_API_KEY`/`INGEST_TOKEN` via `System.getenv`. All of these bypass the `.env` reader, so `-Ddotenv.enabled=false` is harmless and the compose `environment:` block fully configures the app. Credentials/port/DB name in compose (`agentviz`/`agentviz`, `agentbrainviz`, 8080, host `postgres`) match the app defaults and `.env.example`. Correct.
- **`depends_on: condition: service_healthy` ordering is sound** — postgres has a real `pg_isready` healthcheck, and the app tolerates a down DB anyway (`initialization-fail-timeout: -1`, 503s), so nothing races.
- **`pgdata` persistence (the user's core requirement) holds.** Named volume survives `down` and image rebuilds; only `down -v` wipes it. README/compose comments are accurate.
- **No secrets baked or committed.** `.dockerignore` excludes `.env`/`.env.*` (keeps `.env.example`), the build stage's `COPY . .` therefore can't pull `.env` in, and compose uses `${VAR:-}` pass-through with blank defaults. `build/` and `.gradle/` are excluded from context, so no stale-artifact risk, and `clean shadowJar` rebuilds fresh.
- **`.git` exclusion does not break the build** — `Version.VERSION` derives from `GITHUB_REF_NAME` (falls back to `0.1-SNAPSHOT`), not from git, so the missing `.git` only means the in-container version string is `0.1-SNAPSHOT` (cosmetic).
- **`full` profile wiring is correct** — `app` has `profiles: ["full"]`, so bare `docker compose up` stays DB-only. Shadow's default `-all` classifier makes `*-all.jar` an unambiguous match.

### Findings

**1. Runtime container runs as root — no `USER` directive (nit, worth fixing).**
`Dockerfile` runtime stage. The JVM (and thus any code path handling ingested transcript content on an internet-facing 8080) runs as UID 0. `eclipse-temurin:25-jre` does not drop privileges for you. Cheap defense-in-depth: add a non-root user before `ENTRYPOINT`, e.g. `RUN useradd -r -u 10001 app && chown app:app /app/app.jar` then `USER app`. Low real risk for a locally-run tool, but it's a standard Docker hardening rule and costs two lines.
File: `/Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/Dockerfile`

**2. Base images pinned only to floating major tags (nit — reproducibility).**
`FROM eclipse-temurin:25-jdk` / `25-jre` drift as Temurin republishes the tag, so "the same Dockerfile" produces different images over time. For a reproducible build pin to a digest (`eclipse-temurin:25-jre@sha256:…`) or at least a patch tag. Matches the `.dockerignore` comment's own stated goal of "small and reproducible."
File: `/Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/Dockerfile`

**3. `/dev/tcp` healthcheck is a weak readiness signal and couples to the base image (nit).**
`docker-compose.yml` app healthcheck. `bash -c '</dev/tcp/localhost/8080'` works today — Temurin's Ubuntu base ships bash with net-redirection enabled, and Micronaut binds `0.0.0.0:8080` late in startup, so an open port is a reasonable "up" proxy. Two caveats worth noting rather than fixing: (a) it only proves Netty is listening, not that the app is serving (fine here since nothing `depends_on` app health — the check is informational only); (b) it silently breaks if the base image is ever switched to `-alpine` (BusyBox `sh`, no bash) or a bash built without net redirections. An HTTP probe against an actual endpoint would be a stronger readiness check and survive a base-image change.
File: `/Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/docker-compose.yml`

**4. Dockerfile is BuildKit-only; no note of the requirement (nit).**
The `# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1` directive and `--mount=type=cache` both require BuildKit. Modern Docker/`docker compose` default to it, but a user with `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=0` or an old Docker gets a confusing failure. A one-line README aside ("requires Docker with BuildKit, the default since 23.0") would preempt it.
File: `/Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/README.md`

### Docs
README and compose comments are accurate — the persistence/`down` vs `down -v` semantics, the `full`-profile split, host-side `agent-ingest --server http://localhost:8080`, and the file-preview (`/api/brain/file` can't see host `~/.gemini`) limitation are all correctly stated. The "or put it in `.env`" note for `GEMINI_API_KEY` is correct: it works via compose's own `.env` interpolation into `${GEMINI_API_KEY:-}`, independent of the app's disabled dotenv reader.

Nothing here blocks the commit; items 1–2 are the ones I'd fix before it becomes the reference setup.
</output>

gh pr create --base main --head chore/docker-full-stack --title "chore: run the app and database together in Docker" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Why

Adds a first-class way to run the **whole stack — app + Postgres — in Docker**, with the database on a persistent volume, so `docker compose` can bring the site up on any host with no Java or Gradle installed.

## What's in it

- **`Dockerfile`** — two-stage build: `eclipse-temurin:25-jdk` compiles the shadow jar (`gradlew shadowJar`, with a BuildKit cache mount for Gradle deps), then `eclipse-temurin:25-jre` runs it as a **non-root user** (`uid 10001`). Config comes from the environment, so no `.env` is baked in.
- **`.dockerignore`** — keeps build outputs, the Go `cli/`, `node_modules`, and secrets out of the image context.
- **`docker-compose.yml`** — new `app` service behind a `full` profile:
  - `docker compose up -d` → **database only** (unchanged; the default for local dev where the app runs on the host via `./gradlew run`).
  - `docker compose --profile full up -d --build` → **app + database**, with the app connecting to Postgres by service name and waiting on its healthcheck.
- **`README.md`** — full-stack / database-only / backup / teardown instructions, plus the one machine-local caveat (inline file preview can't see the host's `~/.gemini` from inside the container).

## The database is persistent

Postgres data lives in the named `pgdata` volume, decoupled from the container lifecycle. It survives restarts, `docker compose down`, and **app-image rebuilds**; it is wiped only by `docker compose down -v`.

Verified end to end: built the image, ingested a session through the containerized app, ran `docker compose down`, then `up` and a full `--build` rebuild — the session persisted every time, and the app runs as `uid=10001`.

## Notes

- The build requires Docker with BuildKit (default since Docker 23.0).
- Base image tags float on their major version deliberately, so rebuilds pick up JRE security patches; pin a digest if you need bit-reproducible images.
- No app source changed — this is build/infra and docs only.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -2

content.text:
Done — this is set up and working. CI is running on PR #41.

## What I built

**The full stack now runs in Docker**, with the database on a persistent volume:

```bash
docker compose --profile full up -d --build   # app + Postgres, in Docker
```
→ open http://localhost:8080. No Java or Gradle needed on the host — the `Dockerfile` builds the jar inside Docker and runs it on a slim JRE as a non-root user.

**The database persists exactly as you asked.** Its data lives in the named `pgdata` volume, decoupled from the containers, so it survives restarts, `docker compose down`, **and app-image rebuilds** — wiped only by `down -v`. I verified this end to end: ingested a session, tore the stack down, brought it back up, and did a full rebuild — the data was intact each time.

I kept `docker compose up -d` as **database-only** (via a `full` profile), so the existing local-dev workflow (run the app on the host with `./gradlew run`) is unchanged. The README documents full-stack, DB-only, backup (`pg_dump`), and teardown commands.

## Is there a better way than what you suggested?

Your suggestion — compose with site + db and a persistent DB volume — **is** the standard, correct approach, and it's what I implemented. Three refinements I added on top:
1. **Self-contained image build** (multi-stage Dockerfile) so nobody needs a JDK/Gradle to run it.
2. **A `full` profile** so DB-only development still works with a plain `docker compose up`.
3. **Non-root container, healthchecks, service-name networking, ordered startup.**

**The one genuinely-better option for your actual goal (multiple machines):** this all-in-one compose stack is ideal for a *single host* or local use. But for true cross-machine sharing, the strongest setup is to containerize **only the app** and point `DATABASE_URL` at a **managed Postgres** (Neon, Supabase, Cloud SQL). A managed DB gives you automated backups, availability, and one stable endpoint every machine's `agent-ingest` targets — you don't want each laptop hosting its own DB container. The compose file supports that today: run `--profile full` but override `DATABASE_URL` to the managed instance and drop the `postgres` service. I noted this in the PR.

**One honest limitation** (flagged in the README and by the reviewer): the inline **file-preview** feature reads real files under the host's `~/.gemini`, which a container can't see. Everything served from the store — sessions, transcripts, analyses, insights — works normally.

The local full stack is still **running** on your machine (ports 8080 + 5432); `docker compose down` stops it and keeps the data. I'll report once CI on PR #41 is green — then it's ready to merge whenever you'd like (I'll hold for your go-ahead, as before).

content.text:
🎉 **Merged.** PR #41 squash-landed on `main` as `383d4a2`, right on top of the migration (`bac9f57`). The branch is deleted, `main` is synced, and your `.codex`/`.cursor`/`.github/hooks` dirs are still untracked — untouched throughout.

## Done — you can now run everything in Docker

```bash
docker compose --profile full up -d --build   # app + Postgres in Docker
```
→ http://localhost:8080. The database persists in the `pgdata` volume across restarts, `down`, and image rebuilds; only `down -v` wipes it. `docker compose up -d` alone stays database-only for host-side dev.

**On "a better way":** your suggestion was the right one, and I implemented it with a self-contained build, a non-root container, healthchecks, and a `full` profile so DB-only dev still works. The one upgrade for your true multi-machine goal is to containerize just the app and point `DATABASE_URL` at a **managed Postgres** (Neon/Supabase/Cloud SQL) — one durable, backed-up endpoint every machine's `agent-ingest` targets, rather than each host running its own DB. The compose file already supports that (override `DATABASE_URL`, drop the `postgres` service).

## Where things stand overall

Both pieces of work are now on `main`:
- **The migration** (#40) — trajectories in shared Postgres, push-based ingest, the `agent-ingest` CLI, reads served from the store.
- **Docker full-stack** (#41) — one command to run the site + DB with a persistent volume.

The whole review-gated workflow held up across both: the sub-agents caught real bugs before they shipped (the eval key collision, the CLI's UTF-8 hash divergence, the Antigravity analysis-parity trap, and here the root-user container), and everything was verified against the running system, not just mocks.

The local full stack is still up on your machine (ports 8080 + 5432) — `docker compose down` stops it and keeps your data. Anything else you'd like to tackle?
Take a look at the app as it sits today. and go through and suggest anything we are missing.
Let me actually look at the current state rather than go from memory — grounding the assessment in what's really there today.

content.text:
Good — that's a **confirmed** latent bug, not a hypothetical. Here's my assessment, grounded in what's actually in the code today (I verified each claim rather than guessing).

## Top 3 I'd fix first

**1. Ingest rejects payloads over 10 MB — confirmed `413`.** I POSTed a ~12 MB session and got `Request Entity Too Large` (Micronaut's default `max-request-size` is 10 MB). Agent transcripts routinely run tens of MB, and `agent-ingest` batches **100 sessions per request** — so real syncs will silently fail (the CLI reports them as `failed`). This is the one thing most likely to bite a real user on day one.
Fix: raise `micronaut.server.max-request-size`/`max-request-buffer-size` (e.g. 200 MB), **and** make the CLI chunk by byte-size rather than count (and never put two huge sessions in one request). Small, high-value.

**2. The migration left two things half-done:**
- **The CLI never pushes summaries.** In Step 5 I added an optional `summary` to the ingest API specifically so cached analyses could be carried over — but the Go CLI doesn't populate it. So Antigravity's on-disk `summary.json` analyses are **not migrated**; users silently lose them and must re-run (spending tokens). The API side is built; the CLI just needs to read `summary.json`/`short_title.txt` and send them.
- **No real schema migrations.** The schema is still `CREATE ... IF NOT EXISTS`, which *cannot alter an existing table*. The next schema change will silently no-op on anyone's existing DB. Add Flyway (Micronaut has first-class support) before the first schema change — this is the #1 tech-debt item now that it's shipped.

**3. Auth/exposure posture, now that it's Dockerized.** Only `/api/ingest/**` is behind `INGEST_TOKEN`. **`/api/analysis/.../summarize` has no auth and makes real Gemini calls** — anyone who can reach the app can run up your API bill. And the ingest token travels over plain HTTP (no TLS). None of this matters on localhost, but the Docker stack invites putting it on a network. At minimum: document "don't expose without a reverse proxy + HTTPS"; better: gate the costly/mutating endpoints.

## Worth doing soon

- **Native image isn't in PR CI.** `release-native-binaries.yml` builds native binaries at release, but PR CI never does a native compile — so the pgjdbc/Hikari reflection the store added is unverified until release, where it can fail. Add a native smoke-compile + boot to CI.
- **`listConversations` is unbounded.** The sidebar query has no `LIMIT` (cross-session features cap at 150, but the list doesn't). With thousands of sessions that's a huge payload. Add pagination or a cap.
- **No `/health` readiness endpoint.** The Docker healthcheck only checks the port is open, not that the DB is reachable. Adding `micronaut-management` gives a real `/health` that gates `depends_on`/load balancers.

## Polish

- **Empty-store onboarding.** A fresh install shows "No sessions found" with no guidance. Add a hint pointing at `agent-ingest` (ingest is CLI-only, which is fine — but the empty state should teach it).
- **CLI scheduling docs.** The CLI says it's "safe to run on a schedule" but gives no `cron`/`launchd`/`systemd` example.
- **Ad-hoc logging.** `AnalysisController` uses `System.out/System.err`; no structured logging or metrics.

## What's genuinely solid (not gaps)

Test coverage (unit + Testcontainers + Playwright, all against real infra), the ingest dedup/idempotency, the cross-language hash contract, backup docs (`pg_dump`), graceful DB-down handling, and the review-gated workflow. The foundation is strong.

---

My recommendation: **do #1 now** (confirmed bug, small), then **#2** (finish the migration so it's actually complete and lossless). #3 depends on whether you'll expose it beyond localhost.

Want me to take any of these — starting with the 10 MB ingest limit + CLI byte-batching — under the same workflow? I'd size #1 as a small PR, #2 as a medium one.
Ok lets start with 1 then we will do the rest

On it. #1 has two parts: raise the server's request-size limit **and** make the CLI batch by byte-size (so it never builds an oversized request). Following the workflow — branching off main, then TDD.

Review a small change to the `agent-ingest` Go CLI (working dir /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer, branch fix/ingest-large-payloads; see `git diff main -- cli/`).

The change makes request batching byte-aware: `chunk(sessions, maxCount, maxBytes)` now flushes a request when the next session would exceed either the count cap (`--batch-size`, default 100) or a byte budget (`maxBatchBytes` = 16 MiB), and a single session larger than the budget is sent in its own request. This is so a batch of large transcripts can't exceed the server's request-size limit. The `--batch-size` help text was updated: "max sessions per push request (default 100; a request is also capped by total size, and one large session is sent alone)".

The database should have a volume attached so when docker shuts down or a new image is created the database can be reattached. Also add instructions...    - Antigravity NATIVE schema (USER_INPUT/PLANNER_RESPONSE/ERROR_MESSAGE, tool_calls[].arguments — TranscriptParser) vs NORMALIZED adapter schema (FUNCTION_CALL/FUNCTION_OUTPUT, tool_calls[].args — NormalizedSteps); AnalysisSequences.from() dispatches by source
   - Micronaut request limits: max-request-size AND max-request-buffer-size (both default 10MB, both cap buffered body)

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - `cli/main.go` (CURRENT WORK): `const maxBatchBytes = 16 << 20`; refactored `chunk(sessions []client.PushSession, maxCount, maxBytes int) [][]client.PushSession` to flush when count OR byte budget exceeded, oversized session sent alone. Call site: `for _, batch := range chunk(changed, cfg.batchSize, maxBatchBytes)`. Updated --batch-size help text.
   - `cli/main_test.go` (CURRENT WORK): Added `client` import; added TestChunkSplitsByByteBudgetNotJustCount, TestChunkSendsAnOversizedSessionInItsOwnRequest, TestChunkStillRespectsTheCountCap, sizesOf() helper.
   - `src/test/java/io/github/glaforge/agybrainviz/IngestControllerTest.java` (CURRENT WORK): Added acceptsALargeTranscriptOverTheDefaultRequestLimit() — POSTs ~11MB transcript, asserts ingested=1. 5 tests total, all pass.
   - `AnalysisSequences.java` (Step 5, key): `from(source, steps)` dispatches antigravity*→TranscriptParser (steps re-serialized to lines), else→NormalizedSteps. `fromStepsJson(source, stepsJson)` parses then dispatches. Guards analysis parity.
   - `SessionRepository.java`: LIST_SQL uses `COALESCE(NULLIF(m.short_title,''), s.title)` LEFT JOIN summaries; upsert with WHERE content_hash <>; steps/exists/collect/countBySource/manifest/listConversations.
   - `Ingestor.java`: sha256(raw) server-side; stores optional pushed summary via SummaryRepository.
   - `Dockerfile`: 2-stage (eclipse-temurin:25-jdk build shadowJar → 25-jre runtime); non-root `useradd --system --uid 10001 app`, `USER app`; ENTRYPOINT java -Ddotenv.enabled=false -jar app.jar.

4. Errors and fixes:
