gh pr create --base main --head feat/conversations-pagination --title "feat(brain): page the conversation list instead of loading every session" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What

The sidebar fetched **every** session for a source in one response and rendered them all — a big payload and a big DOM for a large store. The list is now paged.

## Server

- `GET /api/brain/conversations` returns a `ConversationPage` `{items, total, limit, offset}` instead of an unbounded array. `SessionRepository.listConversations` takes `limit`/`offset` (`LIMIT ? OFFSET ?`, keeping the stable `updated_at DESC, id` order). `BrainController` clamps `limit` to `[1, 1000]` (default 200) and `offset` to `>= 0`, and reports the source's full `total` via `countBySource`.

## Frontend

- The sidebar loads the first page and offers **"Load more (X of N loaded)"** to append the next; search and sort stay client-side over what's loaded.
- Fixes surfaced in review: a re-render (search, sort, load-more) no longer re-opens and reloads the selected session — **auto-select runs only on the initial load**, the open session's highlight is re-applied without a re-fetch, and **scroll position is preserved**. Focus returns to the rebuilt button after paging, an empty search with more pages says *"load more to keep searching"*, and a **polite live region** announces the loaded/total count. Non-2xx responses surface the error state rather than an empty list.

## Known trade-off

Offset paging can repeat or skip a boundary row if sessions are ingested mid-browse — an accepted trade-off for a sidebar (re-open the flavor to refresh), noted in the code. Keyset pagination is a future option (the `(source, updated_at DESC, id)` index already supports it).

## Tests

Server: `SessionRepositoryTest` (limit/offset paging), `BrainControllerTest` (page shape, total, clamp, offset-past-end). Frontend: `app.test.js` (paged mock, load-more append, empty-search-with-more-pages footer). e2e: `sessions.spec.js` API-paging test; full 33-test Playwright suite green.

## Review

verifier — GREEN (full build + Spotless, Vitest 115, targeted e2e). code-review — no must-fix (offset-drift documented, edge tests added). ui-review — its actionable findings all fixed (the transcript-reload-on-load-more, focus loss, empty-search messaging, live-region).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
## Conversation-list pagination done — [PR #46](https://github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/pull/46)

The sidebar no longer loads every session at once. The endpoint returns a bounded page `{items, total, limit, offset}` (default 200, cap 1000), and the sidebar offers **"Load more (X of N loaded)"** while search/sort stay client-side over what's loaded.

The review pass caught a real bug I'd introduced — "Load more" was flowing through the auto-select tail and **reloading the open transcript + re-firing analysis + resetting scroll**. Fixed: auto-select only on initial load, highlight re-applied without a re-fetch, scroll preserved, focus returned to the button, plus clearer empty-search messaging and an aria-live count.

**Reviews:** verifier GREEN, code-review no must-fix (offset-drift documented, edge tests added), ui-review's findings all fixed. **CI:** all four ✅ (full 33-test Playwright suite passes).

Ready to merge:

content.text: Health tests pass. Now wire the compose healthcheck to the liveness endpoint (HTTP GET via bash, since the JRE image has no curl):

input.old_string:
healthcheck:
      # bash (present in the temurin image) can open a raw TCP socket, so no curl needed.
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "bash -c '</dev/tcp/localhost/8080' || exit 1"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 12
      start_period: 40s
input.new_string:
healthcheck:
      # No curl in the JRE image, so use bash's /dev/tcp to make a minimal HTTP request to the
      # liveness probe and confirm a 200. Liveness (not readiness) on purpose: the app serves the UI
      # even when the store is down, so a database blip must not mark the container unhealthy.
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "bash -c 'exec 3<>/dev/tcp/localhost/8080 && printf \"GET /health HTTP/1.0\\r\\n\\r\\n\" >&3 && grep -q \"200 OK\" <&3'"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 12
      start_period: 40s

input.old_string:
```bash
docker compose ps                          # status + health of both services
docker compose logs -f app                 # follow the app's logs
docker compose exec postgres psql -U agentviz agentbrainviz   # a psql shell into the store
docker compose exec postgres pg_dump -U agentviz agentbrainviz > backup.sql   # back the store up
```

input.new_string:
```bash
docker compose ps                          # status + health of both services
docker compose logs -f app                 # follow the app's logs
docker compose exec postgres psql -U agentviz agentbrainviz   # a psql shell into the store
docker compose exec postgres pg_dump -U agentviz agentbrainviz > backup.sql   # back the store up
```

The app exposes two health probes for a container orchestrator, load balancer, or uptime monitor:

- `GET /health` — **liveness**: `200 {"status":"UP"}` whenever the process is serving. It never
  touches the database (the compose healthcheck uses this), so a store blip doesn't mark the app
  unhealthy — it still serves the UI.
- `GET /health/ready` — **readiness**: `200 {"status":"UP","db":"UP"}` when the store is reachable,
  `503 {"status":"DOWN","db":"DOWN"}` when it isn't, so traffic isn't routed to an app that can't
  serve trajectory data.

<result>I've reviewed the controller, test, docker-compose healthcheck, README, serde setup, and auth filter scope. This is a clean, well-considered change. Findings below.

## Verification of the specific concerns

**1. Readiness logic / resource leak — correct, no leak.**
`try (Connection connection = dataSource.getConnection())` guarantees the connection is returned to Hikari on every path: the `isValid==true` (200) path, the `isValid==false` fall-through to 503, and the `isValid` throwing path (close still runs). On the `getConnection` throw path nothing is assigned, so there's nothing to close. `connection.isValid(2)` is the right call — it uses the driver's lightweight validation rather than running a query. Catching only `SQLException` is adequate: Hikari pool exhaustion / borrow timeout surfaces as `SQLTransientConnectionException` (a `SQLException`), and a shut-down `HikariDataSource` throws `SQLException("… has been closed.")`. A truly unexpected `RuntimeException` would yield a 500 rather than 503, which is acceptable (and arguably correct) for an unforeseen fault.

**2. Unauthenticated probes — correct.** `IngestAuthFilter` is `@ServerFilter("/api/ingest/**")`, so `/health*` is deliberately open. The bodies are only `UP`/`DOWN` plus a `db` flag — no version, hostname, driver, or exception text (the exception is logged at debug, not returned). Nothing sensitive leaks.

**3. Compose healthcheck shell — escaping is correct.** In a YAML double-quoted scalar `\\r\\n` resolves to the literal `\r\n` (backslash-r backslash-n), which `printf` then turns into CR/LF — that's the intended two-step. `CMD-SHELL` runs `/bin/sh -c '<string>'`, and the string is `bash -c '…'`, so `/dev/tcp` (a bash builtin, not sh) is handled by bash. HTTP/1.0 with no Host returns 200 from Netty, and HTTP/1.0 makes the server close the socket → EOF, so `grep` can't hang on a no-match; `grep -q` also exits immediately on match. The 5s `timeout` backstops any stall. Correct and robust.

**4. Native image — safe.** Plain `@Controller` with constructor injection needs no reachability metadata. Return types are `java.util.Map`, natively supported by `micronaut-serde-jackson` (confirmed on the classpath) — no reflective/`@Serdeable` gap. `nativeCompile` won't break.

**5. Micronaut idioms — fine.** Raw `Map` for liveness vs `HttpResponse<Map>` for readiness is a reasonable asymmetry (readiness needs to set the status). `@Controller("/health")` + empty-path `@Get` → `/health`; `/ready` → `/health/ready`; unambiguous. Liveness correctly omits `@ExecuteOn(IO)` since it does no I/O.

## Nits (optional, none blocking)

- **`HealthControllerTest.java:67-71` — the `isValid()==false` branch is untested.** Only the `getConnection`-throws path to 503 is covered. A connection that is borrowed but returns `isValid(2)==false` hits a different line (the fall-through after the `if`) and has no test. Both paths return 503, so this is coverage completeness, not a defect. Consider a second stub whose connection returns `isValid==false`.
- **`HealthControllerTest.java:70` — the 503 test asserts only status, not body.** It never checks `{"status":"DOWN","db":"DOWN"}`, so a regression that returned 503 with a wrong/empty body would pass. Cheap to add `assertEquals("DOWN", …)`.
- **`docker-compose.yml:65` — `grep -q "200 OK"` is unanchored.** Theoretically a non-status-line containing `200 OK` could match, but `/health`'s body is `{"status":"UP"}`, so in practice there's no false positive. Anchoring to `HTTP/1.[01] 200 ` would be marginally stricter; not worth the added escaping.

No correctness, security, resource-leak, or native-image issues. Good to commit once you decide on the nits.

input.command:
git commit -F - <<'EOF'
feat: add liveness and readiness health probes

The container had only a raw TCP check — the port being open said nothing about
whether the app could actually serve. Add real probes for an orchestrator, load
balancer, or uptime monitor.

- GET /health — liveness: 200 {"status":"UP"} whenever the process is serving. It
  never touches the database, because the app is designed to boot and serve the UI
  even when the store is down; failing liveness on a store blip (and having an
  orchestrator kill the container) would be wrong.
- GET /health/ready — readiness: 200 {"status":"UP","db":"UP"} when the store
  validates a connection, else 503 {"status":"DOWN","db":"DOWN"}, so traffic isn't
  routed to an app that can't serve trajectory data.

Written as a small controller rather than pulling in micronaut-management: a JDBC
probe and a flat JSON body are all that's needed, with no added native-image
reflective surface. The compose app healthcheck now makes an HTTP GET to /health
(liveness) via bash's /dev/tcp — the JRE image has no curl — instead of a bare TCP
connect. Both probes are documented in the README.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF

content.text: Local Java is Temurin (no native-image), so I'll rely on CI for the actual compile (the release workflow already proves native builds work there) but validate the **boot + probe shell logic against the JVM jar** locally — the endpoints are identical. Let me add the `native` job to ci.yml:

input.old_string:
- name: Upload Playwright report
        if: ${{ !cancelled() }}
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v7
        with:
          name: playwright-report
          path: playwright-report/
          retention-days: 7
input.new_string:
- name: Upload Playwright report
        if: ${{ !cancelled() }}
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v7
        with:
          name: playwright-report
          path: playwright-report/
          retention-days: 7

  native:
    name: Native image (GraalVM smoke)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Native compilation is slow; give it room.
    timeout-minutes: 45
    # The binary boots against a real store: this job exists to catch GraalVM reachability gaps in the
    # store path (Hikari, pgjdbc connect/SCRAM auth, jsonb round-trip) that unit tests on the JVM miss.
    # Credentials and port match docker-compose.yml, which are also the app's built-in defaults.
    env:
      GITHUB_REF_NAME: native
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:17-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_DB: agentbrainviz
          POSTGRES_USER: agentviz
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: agentviz
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U agentviz -d agentbrainviz"
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 10
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7

      - name: Set up GraalVM 25
        uses: graalvm/setup-graalvm@v1
        with:
          java-version: "25"
          distribution: graalvm
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          cache: gradle

      - name: Compile the native image
        run: ./gradlew nativeCompile --no-daemon

      - name: Boot the binary and smoke-test the store path
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail

          ./build/native/nativeCompile/agy-brain-viz > native-app.log 2>&1 &
          APP_PID=$!
          trap 'echo "--- native app log ---"; cat native-app.log; kill "$APP_PID" 2>/dev/null || true' EXIT

          # Liveness needs no database, so it flips up as soon as the server is serving.
          for _ in $(seq 1 30); do
            if curl -fsS http://localhost:8080/health >/dev/null 2>&1; then break; fi
            sleep 2
          done

          echo "Readiness (Hikari + pgjdbc connect/auth under native):"
          curl -fsS http://localhost:8080/health/ready | grep -q '"db":"UP"'

          cat > smoke-session.json <<'JSON'
          [{"source":"antigravity-cli","id":"native-smoke","sourceMtime":1700000000000,"raw":"{\"type\":\"USER_INPUT\",\"content\":\"hi\"}\n"}]
          JSON

          echo "Ingest one session (normalize + jsonb upsert):"
          curl -fsS -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/ingest/sessions \
            -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d @smoke-session.json | grep -q '"ingested":1'

          echo "List it back (read query + summaries join):"
          curl -fsS "http://localhost:8080/api/brain/conversations?flavor=antigravity-cli" | grep -q 'native-smoke'

          echo "Read its transcript (stored steps):"
          curl -fsS "http://localhost:8080/api/brain/conversations/native-smoke/transcript?flavor=antigravity-cli" | grep -q 'USER_INPUT'

          echo "Native smoke test passed."

pgjdbc 42.7.4 ships its own GraalVM metadata (SCRAM/SASL, driver service), so the native binary should connect — but native runtime reachability is precisely what only a native run (CI) can confirm, which is this job's whole purpose. input.prompt:
Review a GitHub Actions workflow change on branch feat/native-ci (working dir /Users/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer; `git diff main -- .github/workflows/ci.yml`). It adds a `native` job that GraalVM-`nativeCompile`s the app and boots the binary against a Postgres service to smoke-test the store path (catching native reachability gaps).

Focus on workflow/shell correctness (no app code changed):
1. The heredoc inside the `run: |` block — `cat > smoke-session.json <<'JSON' ... JSON`. In a YAML literal block the common indentation is stripped; I verified with a YAML parser that the `JSON` terminator dedents to column 0 and the payload line too. Confirm this is actually correct and the heredoc won't break (quoted `<<'JSON'` needs the terminator at line start).
2. `set -euo pipefail` with a background app process and a `trap ... EXIT` that cats the log and kills the PID — is the trap correct, and will a failed `grep -q` assertion (which exits non-zero under `set -e`) still fire the trap and surface the app log? Any masking (e.g. the `curl ... | grep` pipeline under pipefail — is that the intended failure behavior)?
3. The readiness wait loop (`for _ in $(seq 1 30); do curl -fsS .../health && break; sleep 2; done`) — if the server never comes up, the loop exhausts and the next `curl -fsS .../health/ready` fails the step. Correct? Any infinite-hang risk?
4. Service/config: the Postgres service uses the same creds/port as docker-compose (the app's built-in defaults), so the binary needs no env to connect. Is that assumption sound given the app reads application.yml defaults (localhost:5432, agentviz/agentviz) when DATABASE_URL is unset? The job sets GITHUB_REF_NAME: native — needed?
5. Is `timeout-minutes: 45` reasonable for native compile? Any missing `--no-daemon` or caching concern? Does `graalvm/setup-graalvm@v1` with distribution graalvm / java 25 / github-token match the existing release-native-binaries.yml (which is known to compile successfully)?
6. Any portability/robustness issue in the smoke curls (the ingest payload's escaped-quote/`\n` JSON, grep assertions matching the right response), or a better/standard way to structure this.

Note: the smoke curl sequence was already validated locally against the JVM jar (readiness/ingest/list/transcript all pass); the only CI-specific unknown is native runtime reachability, which this job exists to surface. Report findings ranked by severity. Do not edit.

<result>I've verified every claim against the code and the referenced workflow. Here are my findings.

## Verdict: the workflow change is correct. No actionable defects.

I confirmed each point you raised, plus checked the endpoint paths, query params, and response shapes the smoke curls depend on.

### 1. Heredoc dedent — CONFIRMED correct
Parsing `ci.yml` with a real YAML loader and printing the exact string the shell receives, the payload line and the `JSON` terminator both land at column 0 after the literal-block common-indentation strip:
```
16|cat > smoke-session.json <<'JSON'
17|[{"source":"antigravity-cli",...,"raw":"{\"type\":\"USER_INPUT\",...}\n"}]
18|JSON
```
A quoted `<<'JSON'` requires the terminator with no leading whitespace — it's at line start, so the heredoc closes correctly. Because it's quoted, the `\"` and `\n` are written verbatim (no shell expansion), producing valid JSON where `raw` is a JSON-string-encoded payload. Correct.

### 2. `set -euo pipefail` + trap — correct
- A failed `grep -q` exits non-zero, `set -e` triggers script exit, and the `EXIT` trap fires — bash does not reset `$?` when running an `EXIT` trap (the trailing `kill ... || true` does not overwrite the exit code), so the app log is cat'd **and** the step still fails. Correct.
- `curl -fsS ... | grep` under `pipefail` is the intended behavior: a non-2xx (`-f` → curl non-zero) or a non-matching body (grep non-zero) both fail the pipeline. No masking of the real failure.

**Nit (robustness, low risk here):** `curl -fsS … | grep -q '…'` can theoretically produce a *false failure* under `pipefail` — when `grep -q` matches early it closes the pipe, curl gets SIGPIPE (exit 141), and `pipefail` reports the rightmost *non-zero*, i.e. 141, even though the assertion passed. This does **not** bite here because the responses are tiny (a single smoke session), so curl finishes writing into the pipe buffer and exits 0 before grep closes it. If any of these bodies grew large it could flake. Optional hardening: `resp=$(curl -fsS …); grep -q '…' <<<"$resp"`.

### 3. Readiness loop — correct, no hang
`for _ in $(seq 1 30); … sleep 2` is bounded at ~60s. If the server never serves, the loop exhausts and the subsequent `curl -fsS …/health/ready` fails the step via `set -e`. No infinite-hang path. The `curl` inside the `if` condition correctly does not trip `set -e` during polling. `/health` is liveness (no DB), so it flips up as soon as Netty serves — the right probe to gate on.

### 4. Service creds/port assumption — sound
Postgres service (`agentbrainviz` / `agentviz` / `agentviz`, `5432:5432`) exactly matches the `application.yml` defaults (`jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/agentbrainviz`, `agentviz`/`agentviz`), which `Application.applyDatasourceSettings()` only overrides when `DATABASE_URL` is set. `DATABASE_URL` is unset here, the job runs directly on the runner (not `container:`), so `localhost:5432` maps to the service. Health-gating means Postgres is ready before steps run, and `POSTGRES_DB` creates the database, so schema bootstrap succeeds. No env needed. Correct.

**Nit — `GITHUB_REF_NAME: native` is not strictly needed.** The native binary name is fixed (`build.gradle` sets `imageName = "agy-brain-viz"`, version-independent), so unlike the `backend`/`e2e` jobs — where a `<pr>/merge` slash leaks into the jar filename — nothing in this job's binary path depends on the version. It's harmless and defensive (avoids a slash in the embedded project version during `nativeCompile`, an untested path in `release-native-binaries.yml`), so keeping it is fine; just know it isn't load-bearing.

### 5. `timeout-minutes: 45`, `--no-daemon`, GraalVM setup — good
45 min is generous for `nativeCompile` (typically well under that). `--no-daemon` is present (a slight improvement over the release workflow, which omits it). `graalvm/setup-graalvm@v1` with `distribution: graalvm`, `java-version: "25"`, `github-token`, `cache: gradle` matches the known-good `release-native-binaries.yml` exactly. `cache: gradle` caches Gradle deps, not the native-image build itself — expected; no concern. `actions/checkout@v7` matches the rest of `ci.yml`.

### 6. Smoke curls / assertions — all verified against the code
- Ingest: `IngestResult(int ingested, int skipped, int failed)` is `@Serdeable` → serializes `{"ingested":1,...}`; `grep -q '"ingested":1'` matches.
- Readiness: `HealthController.readiness()` returns `Map.of("status","UP","db","UP")` → `"db":"UP"` (no spaces from Micronaut serde); matches.
- List: `BrainController` `@Get("/conversations")` with `@QueryValue Optional<String> flavor` — path and param name correct.
- Transcript: `@Get("/conversations/{id}/transcript")` with `flavor` query param — correct.

gh pr create --base main --head feat/native-ci --title "ci: add a native-image smoke job that boots the binary against Postgres" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What

The release workflow compiles native binaries but never *runs* one, so a GraalVM reachability gap in the store path (Hikari, pgjdbc connect/SCRAM auth, jsonb round-trip) would ship silently — the JVM tests can't catch it. This adds a CI job that `nativeCompile`s, boots the binary against a Postgres service, and smoke-tests the real path.

## The smoke test

1. **Readiness** (`/health/ready`) — exercises Hikari + pgjdbc connect/auth under native.
2. **Ingest** one session — normalize + `jsonb` upsert.
3. **List** it back — read query + summaries join.
4. **Transcript** read — stored steps.

Modeled on `release-native-binaries.yml` (same GraalVM setup, already known to compile) and the `e2e` job (same Postgres service and default credentials, so the binary connects with no extra config). The smoke assertions capture each response and grep a here-string — echoing bodies into the CI log and avoiding a `pipefail` SIGPIPE flake.

## Validation

- YAML parsed with a real loader; the heredoc terminator dedents to column 0 (verified).
- The **full smoke curl sequence was run locally against the JVM jar** (readiness/ingest/list/transcript all pass with the expected bodies) — the endpoints are identical, so the only CI-specific unknown is native runtime reachability, which is exactly what this job exists to surface.
- `nativeCompile` itself is already proven by the existing release workflow.

## Review

code-review — no actionable defects (heredoc, `set -euo pipefail` + trap, readiness loop, service creds, GraalVM setup all confirmed correct); its SIGPIPE-hardening nit is folded in. This is a CI-only change (no app code), so the authoritative verification is the native job's own run on this PR.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

<configuration>

    <!-- Human-readable, colorized console — the default, and what you want at a dev terminal. -->
    <appender name="CONSOLE_TEXT" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
        <!-- encoders are assigned the type
             ch.qos.logback.classic.encoder.PatternLayoutEncoder by default -->
        <encoder>
            <pattern>%cyan(%d{HH:mm:ss.SSS}) %gray([%thread]) %highlight(%-5level) %magenta(%logger{36}) - %msg%n</pattern>
        </encoder>
    </appender>

    <!-- One JSON object per line, for a log aggregator in production. Uses logback's built-in
         JsonEncoder (1.3.8+), so no extra dependency and no native-image reflective surface. -->
    <appender name="CONSOLE_JSON" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
        <encoder class="ch.qos.logback.classic.encoder.JsonEncoder"/>
    </appender>

    <logger name="io.micronaut.context.DefaultBeanContext" level="ERROR" />

    <!-- Text by default; set LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_JSON (e.g. in production) for structured logs. -->
    <root level="info">
        <appender-ref ref="${LOG_APPENDER:-CONSOLE_TEXT}" />
    </root>
</configuration>

         JsonEncoder (1.3.8+), so no extra dependency and no native-image reflective surface. -->
    <appender name="CONSOLE_JSON" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
        <encoder class="ch.qos.logback.classic.encoder.JsonEncoder"/>
    </appender>

    <logger name="io.micronaut.context.DefaultBeanContext" level="ERROR" />

    <!-- Text by default; set LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_JSON (e.g. in production) for structured logs. -->
    <root level="info">
        <appender-ref ref="${LOG_APPENDER:-CONSOLE_TEXT}" />
    </root>
</configuration>

<result>I have everything I need. Here is my review.

## Review: structured-logging change (branch `feat/structured-logging`)

The diff is small and the JVM behavior is sound. The one thing that matters here is a native-image interaction the diff doesn't mention, and it's exactly what the new CI assertion is (rightly) there to catch. Findings ranked by severity.

---

### 1. ACTIONABLE — `replaceLogbackXml = true` (Micronaut AOT) vs. the runtime `${LOG_APPENDER}` in `appender-ref`: native-only, must confirm the CI job is green before merge

`build.gradle:100` sets `replaceLogbackXml = true`. Under `nativeCompile` (which CI runs at `.github/workflows/ci.yml:178`), Micronaut AOT does **not** ship `logback.xml` — it parses it at build time and generates Java that reconstructs the config programmatically. I decompiled the optimizer (`io.micronaut.aot.std.sourcegen.Logback14GeneratorHelper` in micronaut-aot-std-optimizers 3.0.0): it builds a build-time `Map<String,String> appenderRefToAppenderVarName` keyed by appender `name` (here `CONSOLE_TEXT`, `CONSOLE_JSON`) and wires each root `appender-ref` by looking up `AppenderRefModel.getRef()` in that map. There is no `OptionHelper`/`substVars`/`getenv` call anywhere in those generator classes.

That means the root's `ref="${LOG_APPENDER:-CONSOLE_TEXT}"` is resolved **at image-build time, not at container runtime**, with two possible bad outcomes in the native binary:
- the placeholder is looked up literally and misses the map → the root logger is wired to **no appender** → the native binary emits **zero logs** regardless of `LOG_APPENDER`; or
- it resolves to the build-time default `CONSOLE_TEXT` (CI builds with `LOG_APPENDER` unset) and bakes it → runtime `LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_JSON` is **ignored**, so a native production deploy silently never emits JSON.

Either way the concrete failure is: `LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_JSON ./agy-brain-viz` produces no `"loggerName"` line, and the new assertion at `ci.yml:219` (`grep -q '"loggerName"' native-app.log`) turns the native job **red**.

Scope is native-only. The JVM path (`./gradlew run`) and the Docker `full` profile both use the real `logback.xml` — the `Dockerfile` builds a shadowJar on `eclipse-temurin:25-jre` (not native), so `${LOG_APPENDER}` substitution works there and the container-defaults-to-JSON design is fine. So the feature is validated everywhere except the native binary, and that native path is unverified in this diff.

Action: run the native CI job on this branch and confirm it is green **before commit**. If it fails, the fix is to make appender selection something AOT preserves — e.g. keep a single appender and switch the *encoder*, or gate via a Micronaut logging property/profile rather than an env-selected `appender-ref` — not to loosen the metadata.

This is also the correct answer to your Q2: the CI assertion is the right net, and it is sufficient — do **not** pre-add a `JsonEncoder` reflection entry to `reachability-metadata.json`. If AOT does the replacement, the generated code `new`s `JsonEncoder` directly (no reflection — matching the logback.xml comment's claim). If AOT instead falls back to shipping `logback.xml`, then `JsonEncoder` *is* instantiated reflectively and *would* need metadata that logback 1.5.32 does not ship (I checked the jar — it carries no `native-image`/`reflect-config` at all) and the app doesn't have. The single `"loggerName"` grep covers all of these failure modes, so it's the right guard — but precisely because it's load-bearing, it must be observed passing.

### 2. nit — a misspelled `LOG_APPENDER` silently drops all logging (JVM)

The `${VAR:-default}` syntax is honored by logback and env vars are consulted, so this works. But if someone sets `LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_JASON`, logback logs a one-line `Could not find an appender named [CONSOLE_JASON]` to its status stream and the root ends up with **no appender** — the app then runs with all logging silently suppressed. The default `CONSOLE_TEXT` is safe; there's no clean built-in fallback for a bad explicit value. Worth a one-line caveat in `.env.example`/README that the value must be exactly `CONSOLE_TEXT` or `CONSOLE_JSON`.

### 3. nit — README slightly misdescribes the JSON schema

`README.md` lists the fields as `timestamp, level, loggerName, message, mdc, …`. Confirmed against `JsonEncoder` 1.5.32: `timestamp` is **epoch millis (a number)**, not an ISO string; the human-readable text is under **`formattedMessage`** (there is also a separate raw `message` = the un-interpolated pattern); and every line also carries `sequenceNumber`, `nanoseconds`, `threadName`, and a nested `context` (`name`, `birthdate`, `properties`). This is logback's own schema, **not ECS/Logstash** — fine for stdout-tailing aggregators, but if anything downstream expects `@timestamp`/ECS field names it won't match. `throwable` is present and does carry the stacktrace (nested `cause`/`suppressed`/frames) on errors, `null` otherwise — so error logging is covered. Suggest tightening the field list and adding one clause that the shape is logback-native, not ECS.

### 4. Confirmed clean

- **Q5 formatting:** Spotless (`build.gradle:119`) targets only `java` and `javascript` — `logback.xml` is not covered, so no formatting gate applies. No concern.
- **Q3 two-layer defaults:** app default `CONSOLE_TEXT` + compose default `CONSOLE_JSON` is intentional and documented in `README.md` and the `docker-compose.yml` comment. Consistent (Docker is JVM, so it honors the override). Fine.
- **Q6 CI assertion:** `grep -q '"loggerName"'` matches JsonEncoder's actual key (`LOGGER_ATTR_NAME = "loggerName"`), and Micronaut's INFO-level startup logs guarantee such a line deterministically before the grep runs — not flaky. The only way it fails is the genuine native breakage in finding #1, which is the point.

content.text: Confirmed: defining `CONSOLE_JSON` but not referencing it (in the default text mode — and vice-versa in JSON mode) triggers a logback **WARN**, which dumps ~15 plain-text status lines to stdout on **every** boot. That's noise in text mode and — worse — non-JSON lines polluting the JSON stream in JSON mode. That's a real regression from my change. The fix: register a `NopStatusListener` to suppress logback's benign startup status. Let me add it:

input.old_string:
<configuration>

    <!-- Human-readable, colorized console — the default, and what you want at a dev terminal. -->
input.new_string:
<configuration>

    <!-- Whichever appender isn't selected below is "defined but unreferenced", which logback reports
         as a WARN and then dumps its whole status to the console on every boot — plain-text noise that
         would also pollute the JSON stream. Silence that benign startup status. -->
    <statusListener class="ch.qos.logback.core.status.NopStatusListener" />

    <!-- Human-readable, colorized console — the default, and what you want at a dev terminal. -->

feat: optional structured JSON logging for production

aggregator, selectable at runtime, without a new dependency.

  alongside the existing CONSOLE_TEXT, and the root selects between them via
  ${LOG_APPENDER:-CONSOLE_TEXT}. Text stays the default (right for a dev terminal);
  set LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_JSON for one JSON object per line. A NopStatusListener
  silences the benign "appender not referenced" status logback would otherwise dump
  on every boot (which in JSON mode would pollute the stream).

  LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_TEXT for readable container logs.

  logback.xml at build time (Micronaut's replaceLogbackXml), so it logs the built-in
  text format; the native CI job now asserts the binary still emits startup logs
  (guarding against a bad appender wiring going silent) rather than asserting JSON.
- README and .env.example document LOG_APPENDER (exact values, the logback-native
  JSON schema, and the native caveat).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF

gh pr create --base main --head feat/structured-logging --title "feat: optional structured JSON logging for production" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What

Logs were only the human-readable colorized pattern. This adds a **JSON format** for a log aggregator, selectable at runtime, **without a new dependency** (logback 1.5's built-in `JsonEncoder`).

- `logback.xml` gains a `CONSOLE_JSON` appender alongside the existing `CONSOLE_TEXT`, and the root selects between them via `${LOG_APPENDER:-CONSOLE_TEXT}`. Text stays the default (right for a dev terminal); `LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_JSON` gives one JSON object per line. A `NopStatusListener` silences the benign "appender not referenced" status logback would otherwise dump on every boot (which in JSON mode would pollute the stream).
- The **Docker image defaults to JSON** (it's the production target); override with `LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_TEXT` for readable container logs.
- README + `.env.example` document `LOG_APPENDER` (exact values, the logback-native JSON schema, native caveat).

## Native scope (the important nuance)

The native binary bakes `logback.xml` at build time (Micronaut's `replaceLogbackXml`), so this **runtime switch is a JVM/Docker feature** — the native binary logs the built-in text format. The native CI job now asserts the binary **still emits startup logs** (guarding against a bad appender wiring going silent), rather than asserting JSON.

input.old_string:
The human summary and `--json` go to **stdout**; progress and errors go to **stderr**,

input.new_string:
The human summary and `--json` go to **stdout**; progress and errors go to **stderr**,
so the tool composes cleanly in scripts and pipelines. The `--json` object carries an
`ok` boolean (false when any source failed) alongside per-source counts and an `error`
message for any source that could not be synced, so a script can detect failure without
parsing the exit code.

## Scheduling

Because a re-run uploads only what changed and exits `0` when there's nothing to do,
`agent-ingest` is meant to run unattended on a timer. Two conventions make it well-behaved
in a scheduler:

- Pass `--quiet` so a successful run is silent — the scheduler only hears from it on error
  (progress still goes to stderr, the machine-readable summary to stdout).
- Let the **exit code** drive alerting: non-zero means a source failed to sync.
- Keep `AGENT_INGEST_TOKEN` out of the job definition where you can (an env file with tight
  permissions), since it grants write access to the store.

**cron** (Linux/macOS) — every 30 minutes, token sourced from a private file, output logged:

```cron
*/30 * * * * . "$HOME/.config/agent-ingest/token.env" && /usr/local/bin/agent-ingest \
  --server https://viz.example.com --quiet >> "$HOME/.local/state/agent-ingest.log" 2>&1
```

**systemd timer** (Linux) — a `oneshot` service plus a timer, as user units:

```ini
# ~/.config/systemd/user/agent-ingest.service
[Unit]
Description=Push local agent transcripts to the Agent Brain Visualizer

[Service]
Type=oneshot
Environment=AGENT_INGEST_SERVER=https://viz.example.com
EnvironmentFile=%h/.config/agent-ingest/token.env   # holds AGENT_INGEST_TOKEN=...
ExecStart=%h/bin/agent-ingest --quiet
```

```ini
# ~/.config/systemd/user/agent-ingest.timer
[Unit]
Description=Sync agent transcripts every 30 minutes

[Timer]
OnBootSec=5min
OnUnitActiveSec=30min
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
```

```bash
systemctl --user enable --now agent-ingest.timer
# For runs while you're logged out: sudo loginctl enable-linger "$USER"
```

A failed sync marks the service failed (`systemctl --user status agent-ingest`), which your
usual unit monitoring can pick up.

**launchd** (macOS) — a LaunchAgent that runs every 30 minutes:

```xml
<!-- ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.example.agent-ingest.plist -->
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>Label</key><string>com.example.agent-ingest</string>
  <key>ProgramArguments</key>
  <array>
    <string>/usr/local/bin/agent-ingest</string>
    <string>--quiet</string>
  </array>
  <key>EnvironmentVariables</key>
  <dict>
    <key>AGENT_INGEST_SERVER</key><string>https://viz.example.com</string>
    <key>AGENT_INGEST_TOKEN</key><string>REPLACE_ME</string>
  </dict>
  <key>StartInterval</key><integer>1800</integer>
  <key>RunAtLoad</key><true/>
  <key>StandardErrorPath</key><string>/tmp/agent-ingest.log</string>
</dict>
</plist>
```

```bash
launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.example.agent-ingest.plist
```

launchd has no env file, so the token lives in the plist — keep it readable only by you
(`chmod 600`).

## Test

content.text: Important correction: `progress()` is auto-suppressed when stderr isn't a terminal (line 314), so `--quiet` is largely redundant in any scheduler, and the summary *always* goes to stdout (line 224) — so "--quiet makes a run silent" is inaccurate. Let me fix the prose:

input.old_string:
Because a re-run uploads only what changed and exits `0` when there's nothing to do,
`agent-ingest` is meant to run unattended on a timer. Two conventions make it well-behaved
in a scheduler:

- Pass `--quiet` so a successful run is silent — the scheduler only hears from it on error
  (progress still goes to stderr, the machine-readable summary to stdout).
- Let the **exit code** drive alerting: non-zero means a source failed to sync.
- Keep `AGENT_INGEST_TOKEN` out of the job definition where you can (an env file with tight
  permissions), since it grants write access to the store.
input.new_string:
Because a re-run uploads only what changed and exits `0` when there's nothing to do,
`agent-ingest` is meant to run unattended on a timer. A few things make it well-behaved in a
scheduler:

- The final summary always prints to **stdout** — redirect it to a log file (as below) or to
  `/dev/null`. The per-source progress chatter is silenced automatically whenever output isn't
  a terminal (i.e. under any scheduler), so `--quiet` is optional; it just forces the same on a
  terminal.
- Errors go to **stderr** and set a non-zero **exit code** — wire those to your alerting.
- Keep `AGENT_INGEST_TOKEN` out of the job definition where you can (an env file with tight
  permissions), since it grants write access to the store.
